mentors.name: Ryan Cole
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Ryan Cole directs the Cole Vision & Perception Lab with a focus on 3D scene understanding, integrating learning-based inference with geometric priors when they are trustworthy. Method work spans efficient architectures, training curricula, and post-training calibration for deployed stacks. Applications include assistive technologies, autonomous systems research platforms, and scientific imaging where interpretability and failure visibility matter. Key challenges include domain shift, label noise, and defining realistic evaluation scenarios beyond curated sets. Undergraduates often begin by reproducing baselines on internal datasets, then implement diagnostics (slice analysis, failure clustering); Dr. Dr. Ryan Cole emphasizes milestone demos. Teaching bridges classical vision with contemporary deep learning labs emphasizing debugging and visualization. Near-term directions include robust multimodal perception, efficient training for edge devices, and evaluation tooling for long-tail behaviors. Collaborations extend to neighboring engineering departments, clinics, libraries, and civic partners when projects require domain expertise; students are expected to document assumptions gathered from stakeholders. Community norms emphasize kindness rigor: critiques target claims and methods, not individuals; disagreement is modeled as a productive part of research meetings.
mentors.preferred_background: Prior exposure to CV coursework or robotics perception; interest in both modeling and evaluation protocols.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Ryan Cole publishes in CVPR/ICCV-style venues; Cole Vision & Perception Lab regularly releases datasets and reference training scripts.
mentors.advising_style: Biweekly deep dives plus asynchronous code review; prefers demo-driven progress checks.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~10–14 hours/week depending on dataset construction phases.
mentors.lab_focus: Cole Vision & Perception Lab focuses on perception stacks where visual correctness must be validated, not assumed.

mentors.name: Alice Watson
mentors.department: Department of Electrical Engineering & Computer Science
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Alice Watson directs the Watson Systems Security Group, studying defenses that survive motivated adversaries rather than nominal benchmarks. Methods combine empirical attack surfaces with principled mitigations and measurable residual risk articulation. Applications span campus infrastructure hardening, privacy-preserving analytics, and secure ML deployment pipelines. Key tensions include usability vs. assurance, patch latency vs. verification, and evaluating defenses under adaptive attackers. Undergraduates begin with reproduction of CVE analyses and controlled experiments; Dr. Dr. Alice Watson emphasizes ethics training. Courses integrate threat modeling exercises with hands-on labs in a supervised environment. Roadmap directions include supply-chain transparency tooling, privacy for graph analytics, and robustness of ML systems under prompt injection. The lab tracks emerging ethics guidance from professional societies and updates study protocols when norms evolve. Community norms emphasize kindness rigor: critiques target claims and methods, not individuals; disagreement is modeled as a productive part of research meetings. When hardware dependencies arise, students learn budgeting basics, cluster etiquette, and carbon-aware experiment planning as part of responsible systems citizenship.
mentors.preferred_background: Security mindset: assumes attackers exploit ambiguity; expects precise threat models and cautious claims.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Alice Watson publishes in IEEE S&P/CCS/USENIX Security circles; Watson Systems Security Group collaborates with systems and policy researchers.
mentors.advising_style: Structured threat-model reviews; expects careful logging of assumptions and ethical boundaries.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~10–14 hours/week; lab work may include supervised penetration-testing exercises on isolated testbeds.
mentors.lab_focus: Watson Systems Security Group treats security research as adversarial measurement science.

mentors.name: Jordan King
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Jordan King leads the King Language Technologies Group, focusing on language technologies where grounding, attribution, and evaluation honesty are first-class requirements—not optional polish. Research mixes transformer-era modeling with careful dataset governance, human protocols, and targeted stress tests for brittleness. Applications include decision-support assistants, scientific literature workflows, and accessibility tools with explicit uncertainty handling. Open challenges include contamination-aware benchmarking, multilingual fairness, and scaling supervision without amplifying silent biases. Students typically begin with reproduction and error analysis, then move to benchmark proposals; Dr. Dr. Jordan King emphasizes writing-as-thinking. Courses emphasize NLP evaluation literacy and the sociology of dataset creation. Roadmap themes include retrieval-first architectures, structured reasoning interfaces, and lightweight auditing tools for classroom deployments. The lab tracks emerging ethics guidance from professional societies and updates study protocols when norms evolve. Alumni stay loosely connected through shared Slack archives and annual reunions; returning visitors often co-advise capstone threads tied to earlier codebases. When hardware dependencies arise, students learn budgeting basics, cluster etiquette, and carbon-aware experiment planning as part of responsible systems citizenship.
mentors.preferred_background: Comfortable reading ACL-style papers; cares about evaluation design and responsible deployment narratives.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Jordan King publishes across ACL/EMNLP/NAACL venues; King Language Technologies Group collaborates with HCI and policy scholars on deployment studies.
mentors.advising_style: Paper-style milestone memos; prefers annotated bibliographies early in a project.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~9–13 hours/week with heavier reading loads early on.
mentors.lab_focus: King Language Technologies Group treats language models as systems requiring measurement programs, not single-score comparisons.

mentors.name: Thomas Tran
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Thomas Tran leads the Tran Human-Centered Computing Lab, designing interactive systems where human workflows—not model scores—define success. Research mixes iterative prototyping with controlled studies and field deployments when feasible. Domains include education technologies, developer tools, healthcare workflows, and civic participation interfaces. Challenges include generalizing findings across populations and avoiding 'demo bait' without measurable outcomes. Students often begin with literature syntheses and pilot studies; Dr. Dr. Thomas Tran coaches storytelling for mixed-methods results. Teaching emphasizes design rationales, ethics, and statistical humility. Directions include human-AI teaming interfaces, accessibility-first AI assistants, and governance tooling for classroom deployments. Funding mixes federal grants, industry partnerships, and internal fellowships; undergraduates may contribute to grant reporting artifacts, milestones, and reproducibility supplements when appropriate. Accessibility is treated as an engineering constraint: interfaces, tutorial videos, and written instructions aim for inclusive defaults rather than retrofits. Outreach includes mentoring novice programmers in adjacent majors when projects demand interdisciplinary teams.
mentors.preferred_background: Interest in combining lightweight engineering with rigorous study protocols.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Thomas Tran publishes in CHI/CSCW/UIST venues; Tran Human-Centered Computing Lab collaborates with education and clinical partners under IRB oversight.
mentors.advising_style: Studio-style critiques plus structured study milestones; encourages reflective design notebooks.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~8–12 hours/week; study seasons may require coordinated scheduling with participants.
mentors.lab_focus: Tran Human-Centered Computing Lab emphasizes accountable UX research grounded in participant-centered evidence.

mentors.name: David Wilson
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. David Wilson directs the Wilson Systems Research Lab, building systems where latency tails, resource fairness, and operational observability are engineered—not bolted on after deployment. The group combines kernel/user-space prototyping with rigorous benchmarking and failure injection; students learn to distrust cached conclusions without flamegraphs. Targets include ML training clusters, edge inference stacks, and campus-scale research platforms requiring reliability. Core challenges include tail latency under contention, safe upgrades in distributed settings, and reproducible performance evaluation. Undergraduates often start with instrumented micro-benchmarks and bug fixes; Dr. Dr. David Wilson emphasizes incremental commits and design docs. Teaching emphasizes OS internals, concurrency reasoning, and performance culture grounded in measurement. Roadmap themes include systems support for large-model training, energy-aware scheduling, and verified rollback tooling. Alumni stay loosely connected through shared Slack archives and annual reunions; returning visitors often co-advise capstone threads tied to earlier codebases. Outreach includes mentoring novice programmers in adjacent majors when projects demand interdisciplinary teams. The group actively participates in department reading groups, artifact reviews, and undergraduate research symposia so students experience the social norms of scientific criticism—not only isolated coding tasks.
mentors.preferred_background: Strong systems programming maturity and patience for long debugging cycles; coursework in OS + networking helps substantially.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. David Wilson publishes in OSDI/SOSP/NSDI-class venues; Wilson Systems Research Lab ships artifacts used by partner research groups.
mentors.advising_style: Issue-tracker driven development; expects design notes before large refactors.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~12–18 hours/week during kernel-heavy phases; schedules adjust around compile/debug cycles.
mentors.lab_focus: Wilson Systems Research Lab treats systems research as principled engineering with scientific measurement obligations.

mentors.name: Jason Bailey
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Jason Bailey directs the Bailey Research Group on Decision Systems, focusing on representation learning pipelines where scale, compute budgets, and evaluation integrity must be co-designed rather than treated as afterthoughts. Recent technical themes include structured latent variables, representation alignment under distribution shift, and practical tooling for reproducible experimentation. Impact is pursued through open benchmarks, reference implementations, and documentation aimed at lowering the barrier for responsible adoption. The group invests heavily in measurement: defining tasks beyond single-number scores, auditing slices, and studying intervention sensitivity. Mentorship emphasizes readable code, experiment manifests, and crisp writeups—skills that transfer regardless of whether a project ends in a publication or a strong technical report. Teaching interfaces include graduate seminars on probabilistic ML and undergraduate courses that foreground debugging culture and scientific communication. Near-term directions include richer evaluation for generative systems, improved protocols for human-in-the-loop supervision, and tooling that makes robust experimentation teachable—not only feasible for experts. Funding mixes federal grants, industry partnerships, and internal fellowships; undergraduates may contribute to grant reporting artifacts, milestones, and reproducibility supplements when appropriate. Career mentoring includes guidance on graduate school timing, portfolio construction, and translating research experience into interviews—without guaranteeing outcomes. The lab maintains explicit policies on authorship credit, noting that meaningful intellectual contributions—not  merely routine engineering—guide ordering conversations.
mentors.preferred_background: Students who can translate vague ideas into measurable hypotheses, document failures, and iterate with statistical discipline.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Jason Bailey has published extensively in top ML venues; the Bailey Research Group on Decision Systems routinely releases artifacts and participates in community benchmarking efforts.
mentors.advising_style: Hands-on debugging sessions early in a project, transitioning to student-led ownership after Milestone 1.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~8–12 hours/week for coursework-heavy terms, with flexible scheduling around exams.
mentors.lab_focus: Bailey Research Group on Decision Systems emphasizes reproducible ML research with measurable claims and collaborative tooling.

mentors.name: Harper Jenkins
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Harper Jenkins runs the Jenkins Lab for Adaptive ML with an emphasis on empirical ML that still respects identifiability constraints, proper ablations, and honest reporting of negative results. The research program mixes large-scale training insights with smaller diagnostic studies that isolate failure modes in optimization, generalization, or calibration. Impact is pursued through open benchmarks, reference implementations, and documentation aimed at lowering the barrier for responsible adoption. The group invests heavily in measurement: defining tasks beyond single-number scores, auditing slices, and studying intervention sensitivity. Mentorship emphasizes readable code, experiment manifests, and crisp writeups—skills that transfer regardless of whether a project ends in a publication or a strong technical report. Course connections emphasize bridging mathematical intuition with hands-on labs that mirror research workflows. Students interested in interdisciplinary projects should expect to spend time understanding domain constraints before proposing model changes. International readers are considered default: documentation strives for precise terminology, neutral examples, and careful handling of culturally sensitive applications. Funding mixes federal grants, industry partnerships, and internal fellowships; undergraduates may contribute to grant reporting artifacts, milestones, and reproducibility supplements when appropriate. Software stewardship matters: repositories include contribution guidelines, pinned environments, and archived experiment snapshots so future students can continue rather than restart.
mentors.preferred_background: Students who can translate vague ideas into measurable hypotheses, document failures, and iterate with statistical discipline.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Harper Jenkins advises thesis work spanning theory-informed empirical ML and applied collaborations; alumni have joined both academic labs and industry research teams.

mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~10–14 focused hours/week during steady semesters; heavier spikes near deadlines are avoided via earlier checkpoints.

mentors.name: Matthew Martinez
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Matthew Martinez directs the Martinez Security & Privacy Lab, studying defenses that survive motivated adversaries rather than nominal benchmarks. Methods combine empirical attack surfaces with principled mitigations and measurable residual risk articulation. Applications span campus infrastructure hardening, privacy-preserving analytics, and secure ML deployment pipelines. Key tensions include usability vs. assurance, patch latency vs. verification, and evaluating defenses under adaptive attackers. Undergraduates begin with reproduction of CVE analyses and controlled experiments; Dr. Dr. Matthew Martinez emphasizes ethics training. Courses integrate threat modeling exercises with hands-on labs in a supervised environment. Roadmap directions include supply-chain transparency tooling, privacy for graph analytics, and robustness of ML systems under prompt injection. Where feasible, projects publish negative results, ablation catalogs, and limitation sections that later cohorts can build upon without repeating dead ends. The group actively participates in department reading groups, artifact reviews, and undergraduate research symposia so students experience the social norms of scientific criticism—not only isolated coding tasks.

mentors.name: Julia Wright
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Julia Wright directs the Wright Lab for 3D Understanding with a focus on 3D scene understanding, integrating learning-based inference with geometric priors when they are trustworthy. Method work spans efficient architectures, training curricula, and post-training calibration for deployed stacks. Applications include assistive technologies, autonomous systems research platforms, and scientific imaging where interpretability and failure visibility matter. Key challenges include domain shift, label noise, and defining realistic evaluation scenarios beyond curated sets. Undergraduates often begin by reproducing baselines on internal datasets, then implement diagnostics (slice analysis, failure clustering); Dr. Dr. Julia Wright emphasizes milestone demos. Teaching bridges classical vision with contemporary deep learning labs emphasizing debugging and visualization. Near-term directions include robust multimodal perception, efficient training for edge devices, and evaluation tooling for long-tail behaviors. The lab maintains explicit policies on authorship credit, noting that meaningful intellectual contributions—not  merely routine engineering—guide ordering conversations. Where feasible, projects publish negative results, ablation catalogs, and limitation sections that later cohorts can build upon without repeating dead ends.
mentors.preferred_background: Strong linear algebra and comfort with probability; willingness to build data pipelines and sanity-check visual outputs.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Julia Wright publishes in CVPR/ICCV-style venues; Wright Lab for 3D Understanding regularly releases datasets and reference training scripts.
mentors.advising_style: Biweekly deep dives plus asynchronous code review; prefers demo-driven progress checks.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~10–14 hours/week depending on dataset construction phases.
mentors.lab_focus: Wright Lab for 3D Understanding focuses on perception stacks where visual correctness must be validated, not assumed.

mentors.name: Isabella Butler
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Isabella Butler leads the Butler Lab for Discrete Structures & Optimization, investigating fundamental computation limits and designing algorithms with provable guarantees under realistic constraints. Research blends classical discrete optimization with modern probabilistic tools and structured instance analyses. Motivating domains include routing, scheduling, learning-adjacent subroutines, and resource allocation where constants and logarithmic factors genuinely matter. Students confront tension between worst-case guarantees and empirical structure—projects clarify assumptions explicitly. Mentorship emphasizes lemma notebooks, proof sketches before details, and seminar-style presentations; Dr. Dr. Isabella Butler coaches undergraduates toward incremental publishable lemmas when appropriate. Teaching stresses mathematical communication and proof hygiene alongside algorithm design patterns. Directions include dynamic graph algorithms under partial observability, approximation schemes for structured clustering, and bridges to ML theory. Where feasible, projects publish negative results, ablation catalogs, and limitation sections that later cohorts can build upon without repeating dead ends. International readers are considered default: documentation strives for precise terminology, neutral examples, and careful handling of culturally sensitive applications. Outreach includes mentoring novice programmers in adjacent majors when projects demand interdisciplinary teams.
mentors.preferred_background: Comfortable with rigorous proofs and iterative refinement of definitions; expects clarity over speed.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Isabella Butler publishes in STOC/FOCS/SODA-class venues; Butler Lab for Discrete Structures & Optimization welcomes careful undergraduate collaborators on theory-meets-systems bridges.
mentors.advising_style: Weekly proof meetings with collaborative whiteboarding and explicit dependency graphs for lemmas.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~8–12 hours/week for theory-heavy reading and problem sessions.
mentors.lab_focus: Butler Lab for Discrete Structures & Optimization foregrounds crisp problem formulation and honest theorem statements.

mentors.name: Sophia Adams
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Sophia Adams runs the Center-scale ML Research Studio (Adams) with an emphasis on empirical ML that still respects identifiability constraints, proper ablations, and honest reporting of negative results. Recent technical themes include structured latent variables, representation alignment under distribution shift, and practical tooling for reproducible experimentation. Impact is pursued through open benchmarks, reference implementations, and documentation aimed at lowering the barrier for responsible adoption. A recurring challenge is separating incidental correlations from stable mechanisms—students are encouraged to treat surprising gains as hypotheses that demand mechanism-level scrutiny. Mentorship emphasizes readable code, experiment manifests, and crisp writeups—skills that transfer regardless of whether a project ends in a publication or a strong technical report. Course connections emphasize bridging mathematical intuition with hands-on labs that mirror research workflows. Students interested in interdisciplinary projects should expect to spend time understanding domain constraints before proposing model changes. Outreach includes mentoring novice programmers in adjacent majors when projects demand interdisciplinary teams. Career mentoring includes guidance on graduate school timing, portfolio construction, and translating research experience into interviews—without guaranteeing outcomes. mentors.name: Christopher Cooper
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Christopher Cooper directs the Cooper Security & Privacy Lab, studying defenses that survive motivated adversaries rather than nominal benchmarks. Methods combine empirical attack surfaces with principled mitigations and measurable residual risk articulation. Applications span campus infrastructure hardening, privacy-preserving analytics, and secure ML deployment pipelines. Key tensions include usability vs. assurance, patch latency vs. verification, and evaluating defenses under adaptive attackers. Undergraduates begin with reproduction of CVE analyses and controlled experiments; Dr. Dr. Christopher Cooper emphasizes ethics training. Courses integrate threat modeling exercises with hands-on labs in a supervised environment. Roadmap directions include supply-chain transparency tooling, privacy for graph analytics, and robustness of ML systems under prompt injection. International readers are considered default: documentation strives for precise terminology, neutral examples, and careful handling of culturally sensitive applications. mentors.name: Sofia Bailey
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Sofia Bailey runs the Bailey Lab for Adaptive ML with an emphasis on empirical ML that still respects identifiability constraints, proper ablations, and honest reporting of negative results. Recent technical themes include structured latent variables, representation alignment under distribution shift, and practical tooling for reproducible experimentation. Application targets span scientific computing assistants, decision-support tools, and safety-critical settings where uncertainty communication matters as much as raw accuracy. The group invests heavily in measurement: defining tasks beyond single-number scores, auditing slices, and studying intervention sensitivity. Mentorship emphasizes readable code, experiment manifests, and crisp writeups—skills that transfer regardless of whether a project ends in a publication or a strong technical report. Teaching interfaces include graduate seminars on probabilistic ML and undergraduate courses that foreground debugging culture and scientific communication. Near-term directions include richer evaluation for generative systems, improved protocols for human-in-the-loop supervision, and tooling that makes robust experimentation teachable—not only feasible for experts. Funding mixes federal grants, industry partnerships, and internal fellowships; undergraduates may contribute to grant reporting artifacts, milestones, and reproducibility supplements when appropriate. The lab maintains explicit policies on authorship credit, noting that meaningful intellectual contributions—not  merely routine engineering—guide ordering conversations.

mentors.name: Stephen Bryant
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Stephen Bryant leads the Center-scale ML Research Studio (Bryant) and studies learning algorithms that remain reliable when data are biased, partially labeled, or collected under shifting conditions. Methodologically, the group combines modern deep architectures with careful probabilistic framing, stress-testing claims through synthetic controls and targeted interventions rather than leaderboard chasing alone. The lab collaborates with campus partners on climate modeling workflows, biomedical signal pipelines, and education technologies where data governance is non-negotiable. The group invests heavily in measurement: defining tasks beyond single-number scores, auditing slices, and studying intervention sensitivity. Undergraduate researchers typically begin with reproduction and auditing tasks, then graduate to proposing extensions; Dr. Dr. Stephen Bryant prioritizes weekly technical checkpoints and written milestone notes. Teaching interfaces include graduate seminars on probabilistic ML and undergraduate courses that foreground debugging culture and scientific communication. Students interested in interdisciplinary projects should expect to spend time understanding domain constraints before proposing model changes. Career mentoring includes guidance on graduate school timing, portfolio construction, and translating research experience into interviews—without guaranteeing outcomes. International readers are considered default: documentation strives for precise terminology, neutral examples, and careful handling of culturally sensitive applications. When hardware dependencies arise, students learn budgeting basics, cluster etiquette, and carbon-aware experiment planning as part of responsible systems citizenship.

mentors.name: Joshua Watson
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Joshua Watson leads the Watson Algorithms & Theory Group, investigating fundamental computation limits and designing algorithms with provable guarantees under realistic constraints. Research blends classical discrete optimization with modern probabilistic tools and structured instance analyses. Motivating domains include routing, scheduling, learning-adjacent subroutines, and resource allocation where constants and logarithmic factors genuinely matter. Students confront tension between worst-case guarantees and empirical structure—projects clarify assumptions explicitly. Mentorship emphasizes lemma notebooks, proof sketches before details, and seminar-style presentations; Dr. Dr. Joshua Watson coaches undergraduates toward incremental publishable lemmas when appropriate. Teaching stresses mathematical communication and proof hygiene alongside algorithm design patterns. Directions include dynamic graph algorithms under partial observability, approximation schemes for structured clustering, and bridges to ML theory. Community norms emphasize kindness rigor: critiques target claims and methods, not individuals; disagreement is modeled as a productive part of research meetings. Collaborations extend to neighboring engineering departments, clinics, libraries, and civic partners when projects require domain expertise; students are expected to document assumptions gathered from stakeholders.
mentors.preferred_background: Comfortable with rigorous proofs and iterative refinement of definitions; expects clarity over speed.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Joshua Watson publishes in STOC/FOCS/SODA-class venues; Watson Algorithms & Theory Group welcomes careful undergraduate collaborators on theory-meets-systems bridges.
mentors.advising_style: Weekly proof meetings with collaborative whiteboarding and explicit dependency graphs for lemmas.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~8–12 hours/week for theory-heavy reading and problem sessions.
mentors.lab_focus: Watson Algorithms & Theory Group foregrounds crisp problem formulation and honest theorem statements.

mentors.name: Lucas Wright
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Lucas Wright leads the Wright Vision & Perception Lab, developing perception models that remain dependable across lighting, sensor noise, and deployment constraints common in mobile and robotics settings. Method work spans efficient architectures, training curricula, and post-training calibration for deployed stacks. Applications include assistive technologies, autonomous systems research platforms, and scientific imaging where interpretability and failure visibility matter. Key challenges include domain shift, label noise, and defining realistic evaluation scenarios beyond curated sets. Undergraduates often begin by reproducing baselines on internal datasets, then implement diagnostics (slice analysis, failure clustering); Dr. Dr. Lucas Wright emphasizes milestone demos. Teaching bridges classical vision with contemporary deep learning labs emphasizing debugging and visualization. Near-term directions include robust multimodal perception, efficient training for edge devices, and evaluation tooling for long-tail behaviors. The lab tracks emerging ethics guidance from professional societies and updates study protocols when norms evolve. Where feasible, projects publish negative results, ablation catalogs, and limitation sections that later cohorts can build upon without repeating dead ends.
mentors.preferred_background: Prior exposure to CV coursework or robotics perception; interest in both modeling and evaluation protocols.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Lucas Wright publishes in CVPR/ICCV-style venues; Wright Vision & Perception Lab regularly releases datasets and reference training scripts.
mentors.advising_style: Biweekly deep dives plus asynchronous code review; prefers demo-driven progress checks.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~10–14 hours/week depending on dataset construction phases.
mentors.lab_focus: Wright Vision & Perception Lab focuses on perception stacks where visual correctness must be validated, not assumed.

mentors.name: Christopher Young
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Christopher Young directs the Young Security & Privacy Lab, studying defenses that survive motivated adversaries rather than nominal benchmarks. Methods combine empirical attack surfaces with principled mitigations and measurable residual risk articulation. Applications span campus infrastructure hardening, privacy-preserving analytics, and secure ML deployment pipelines. Key tensions include usability vs. assurance, patch latency vs. verification, and evaluating defenses under adaptive attackers. Undergraduates begin with reproduction of CVE analyses and controlled experiments; Dr. Dr. Christopher Young emphasizes ethics training. Courses integrate threat modeling exercises with hands-on labs in a supervised environment. Roadmap directions include supply-chain transparency tooling, privacy for graph analytics, and robustness of ML systems under prompt injection. Community norms emphasize kindness rigor: critiques target claims and methods, not individuals; disagreement is modeled as a productive part of research meetings. Outreach includes mentoring novice programmers in adjacent majors when projects demand interdisciplinary teams. When hardware dependencies arise, students learn budgeting basics, cluster etiquette, and carbon-aware experiment planning as part of responsible systems citizenship.
mentors.preferred_background: Security mindset: assumes attackers exploit ambiguity; expects precise threat models and cautious claims.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Christopher Young publishes in IEEE S&P/CCS/USENIX Security circles; Young Security & Privacy Lab collaborates with systems and policy researchers.
mentors.advising_style: Structured threat-model reviews; expects careful logging of assumptions and ethical boundaries.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~10–14 hours/week; lab work may include supervised penetration-testing exercises on isolated testbeds.

mentors.name: Zara Howard
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Zara Howard leads the Laboratory for Reliable Learning and studies learning algorithms that remain reliable when data are biased, partially labeled, or collected under shifting conditions. Methodologically, the group combines modern deep architectures with careful probabilistic framing, stress-testing claims through synthetic controls and targeted interventions rather than leaderboard chasing alone. The lab collaborates with campus partners on climate modeling workflows, biomedical signal pipelines, and education technologies where data governance is non-negotiable. A recurring challenge is separating incidental correlations from stable mechanisms—students are encouraged to treat surprising gains as hypotheses that demand mechanism-level scrutiny. mentors.name: Peter Nguyen
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Peter Nguyen directs the Nguyen Lab for 3D Understanding with a focus on 3D scene understanding, integrating learning-based inference with geometric priors when they are trustworthy. The group combines modern representation learning with classical geometry and careful dataset audits; students learn to question shortcuts like overly tidy benchmarks. Applications include assistive technologies, autonomous systems research platforms, and scientific imaging where interpretability and failure visibility matter. Key challenges include domain shift, label noise, and defining realistic evaluation scenarios beyond curated sets. Undergraduates often begin by reproducing baselines on internal datasets, then implement diagnostics (slice analysis, failure clustering); Dr. Dr. Peter Nguyen emphasizes milestone demos. Teaching bridges classical vision with contemporary deep learning labs emphasizing debugging and visualization. Near-term directions include robust multimodal perception, efficient training for edge devices, and evaluation tooling for long-tail behaviors. Career mentoring includes guidance on graduate school timing, portfolio construction, and translating research experience into interviews—without guaranteeing outcomes. Outreach includes mentoring novice programmers in adjacent majors when projects demand interdisciplinary teams. Accessibility is treated as an engineering constraint: interfaces, tutorial videos, and written instructions aim for inclusive defaults rather than retrofits.
mentors.preferred_background: Prior exposure to CV coursework or robotics perception; interest in both modeling and evaluation protocols.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Peter Nguyen publishes in CVPR/ICCV-style venues; Nguyen Lab for 3D Understanding regularly releases datasets and reference training scripts.
mentors.advising_style: Biweekly deep dives plus asynchronous code review; prefers demo-driven progress checks.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~10–14 hours/week depending on dataset construction phases.

mentors.name: Maria Ward
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Maria Ward directs the Ward High-Performance Computing Group, building systems where latency tails, resource fairness, and operational observability are engineered—not bolted on after deployment. The group combines kernel/user-space prototyping with rigorous benchmarking and failure injection; students learn to distrust cached conclusions without flamegraphs. Targets include ML training clusters, edge inference stacks, and campus-scale research platforms requiring reliability. Core challenges include tail latency under contention, safe upgrades in distributed settings, and reproducible performance evaluation. Undergraduates often start with instrumented micro-benchmarks and bug fixes; Dr. Dr. Maria Ward emphasizes incremental commits and design docs. Teaching emphasizes OS internals, concurrency reasoning, and performance culture grounded in measurement. Roadmap themes include systems support for large-model training, energy-aware scheduling, and verified rollback tooling. The lab tracks emerging ethics guidance from professional societies and updates study protocols when norms evolve. Community norms emphasize kindness rigor: critiques target claims and methods, not individuals; disagreement is modeled as a productive part of research meetings.
mentors.preferred_background: Students comfortable reading OSDI/SOSP-style papers and annotating design tradeoffs.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Maria Ward publishes in OSDI/SOSP/NSDI-class venues; Ward High-Performance Computing Group ships artifacts used by partner research groups.
mentors.advising_style: Issue-tracker driven development; expects design notes before large refactors.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~12–18 hours/week during kernel-heavy phases; schedules adjust around compile/debug cycles.
mentors.lab_focus: Ward High-Performance Computing Group treats systems research as principled engineering with scientific measurement obligations.

mentors.name: Alice Hill
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Alice Hill leads the Hill Language Technologies Group, focusing on language technologies where grounding, attribution, and evaluation honesty are first-class requirements—not optional polish. Research mixes transformer-era modeling with careful dataset governance, human protocols, and targeted stress tests for brittleness. Applications include decision-support assistants, scientific literature workflows, and accessibility tools with explicit uncertainty handling. Open challenges include contamination-aware benchmarking, multilingual fairness, and scaling supervision without amplifying silent biases. Students typically begin with reproduction and error analysis, then move to benchmark proposals; Dr. Dr. Alice Hill emphasizes writing-as-thinking. Courses emphasize NLP evaluation literacy and the sociology of dataset creation. Roadmap themes include retrieval-first architectures, structured reasoning interfaces, and lightweight auditing tools for classroom deployments. Software stewardship matters: repositories include contribution guidelines, pinned environments, and archived experiment snapshots so future students can continue rather than restart. The group actively participates in department reading groups, artifact reviews, and undergraduate research symposia so students experience the social norms of scientific criticism—not only isolated coding tasks.

mentors.name: Ryan Tran
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Ryan Tran leads the Tran Lab for Discrete Structures & Optimization, investigating fundamental computation limits and designing algorithms with provable guarantees under realistic constraints. Research blends classical discrete optimization with modern probabilistic tools and structured instance analyses. Motivating domains include routing, scheduling, learning-adjacent subroutines, and resource allocation where constants and logarithmic factors genuinely matter. Students confront tension between worst-case guarantees and empirical structure—projects clarify assumptions explicitly. Mentorship emphasizes lemma notebooks, proof sketches before details, and seminar-style presentations; Dr. Dr. Ryan Tran coaches undergraduates toward incremental publishable lemmas when appropriate. Teaching stresses mathematical communication and proof hygiene alongside algorithm design patterns. Directions include dynamic graph algorithms under partial observability, approximation schemes for structured clustering, and bridges to ML theory. When hardware dependencies arise, students learn budgeting basics, cluster etiquette, and carbon-aware experiment planning as part of responsible systems citizenship. Funding mixes federal grants, industry partnerships, and internal fellowships; undergraduates may contribute to grant reporting artifacts, milestones, and reproducibility supplements when appropriate.

mentors.name: Thomas Ross
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Thomas Ross runs the Laboratory for Reliable Learning with an emphasis on empirical ML that still respects identifiability constraints, proper ablations, and honest reporting of negative results. Methodologically, the group combines modern deep architectures with careful probabilistic framing, stress-testing claims through synthetic controls and targeted interventions rather than leaderboard chasing alone. Application targets span scientific computing assistants, decision-support tools, and safety-critical settings where uncertainty communication matters as much as raw accuracy. Another emphasis is compute-aware research: understanding when scaling helps, when it obscures bugs, and how to prioritize experiments under deadline pressure. Mentorship emphasizes readable code, experiment manifests, and crisp writeups—skills that transfer regardless of whether a project ends in a publication or a strong technical report. Teaching interfaces include graduate seminars on probabilistic ML and undergraduate courses that foreground debugging culture and scientific communication. Near-term directions include richer evaluation for generative systems, improved protocols for human-in-the-loop supervision, and tooling that makes robust experimentation teachable—not only feasible for experts. Collaborations extend to neighboring engineering departments, clinics, libraries, and civic partners when projects require domain expertise; students are expected to document assumptions gathered from stakeholders. Alumni stay loosely connected through shared Slack archives and annual reunions; returning visitors often co-advise capstone threads tied to earlier codebases.
mentors.preferred_background: Strong programming maturity and careful experimental habits; comfortable reading NeurIPS/ICML-style papers and reproducing baselines.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Thomas Ross has published extensively in top ML venues; the Laboratory for Reliable Learning routinely releases artifacts and participates in community benchmarking efforts.
mentors.advising_style: Hands-on debugging sessions early in a project, transitioning to student-led ownership after Milestone 1.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~8–12 hours/week for coursework-heavy terms, with flexible scheduling around exams.

mentors.name: Anna Alexander
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Anna Alexander leads the Alexander Interactive Systems Group, designing interactive systems where human workflows—not model scores—define success. Research mixes iterative prototyping with controlled studies and field deployments when feasible. Domains include education technologies, developer tools, healthcare workflows, and civic participation interfaces. Challenges include generalizing findings across populations and avoiding 'demo bait' without measurable outcomes. Students often begin with literature syntheses and pilot studies; Dr. Dr. Anna Alexander coaches storytelling for mixed-methods results. Teaching emphasizes design rationales, ethics, and statistical humility. Directions include human-AI teaming interfaces, accessibility-first AI assistants, and governance tooling for classroom deployments. The group actively participates in department reading groups, artifact reviews, and undergraduate research symposia so students experience the social norms of scientific criticism—not only isolated coding tasks. The lab maintains explicit policies on authorship credit, noting that meaningful intellectual contributions—not  merely routine engineering—guide ordering conversations.
mentors.preferred_background: Comfort translating qualitative insights into design iterations; respects participant consent and privacy.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Anna Alexander publishes in CHI/CSCW/UIST venues; Alexander Interactive Systems Group collaborates with education and clinical partners under IRB oversight.
mentors.advising_style: Studio-style critiques plus structured study milestones; encourages reflective design notebooks.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~8–12 hours/week; study seasons may require coordinated scheduling with participants.

mentors.name: Camila Cox
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Camila Cox runs the Laboratory for Reliable Learning with an emphasis on empirical ML that still respects identifiability constraints, proper ablations, and honest reporting of negative results. Recent technical themes include structured latent variables, representation alignment under distribution shift, and practical tooling for reproducible experimentation. Another emphasis is compute-aware research: understanding when scaling helps, when it obscures bugs, and how to prioritize experiments under deadline pressure. Undergraduate researchers typically begin with reproduction and auditing tasks, then graduate to proposing extensions; Dr. Dr. Camila Cox prioritizes weekly technical checkpoints and written milestone notes. Teaching interfaces include graduate seminars on probabilistic ML and undergraduate courses that foreground debugging culture and scientific communication. Students interested in interdisciplinary projects should expect to spend time understanding domain constraints before proposing model changes. Software stewardship matters: repositories include contribution guidelines, pinned environments, and archived experiment snapshots so future students can continue rather than restart. The lab maintains explicit policies on authorship credit, noting that meaningful intellectual contributions—not  merely routine engineering—guide ordering conversations.

mentors.name: Anna Cole
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Anna Cole leads the Cole Lab for Discrete Structures & Optimization, investigating fundamental computation limits and designing algorithms with provable guarantees under realistic constraints. Research blends classical discrete optimization with modern probabilistic tools and structured instance analyses. Motivating domains include routing, scheduling, learning-adjacent subroutines, and resource allocation where constants and logarithmic factors genuinely matter. Students confront tension between worst-case guarantees and empirical structure—projects clarify assumptions explicitly. Mentorship emphasizes lemma notebooks, proof sketches before details, and seminar-style presentations; Dr. Dr. Anna Cole coaches undergraduates toward incremental publishable lemmas when appropriate. Teaching stresses mathematical communication and proof hygiene alongside algorithm design patterns. Directions include dynamic graph algorithms under partial observability, approximation schemes for structured clustering, and bridges to ML theory. When hardware dependencies arise, students learn budgeting basics, cluster etiquette, and carbon-aware experiment planning as part of responsible systems citizenship. Students are encouraged to present at internal seminars before external deadlines, practicing clarity under questions from faculty outside their immediate specialty.
mentors.preferred_background: Comfortable with rigorous proofs and iterative refinement of definitions; expects clarity over speed.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Anna Cole publishes in STOC/FOCS/SODA-class venues; Cole Lab for Discrete Structures & Optimization welcomes careful undergraduate collaborators on theory-meets-systems bridges.
mentors.advising_style: Weekly proof meetings with collaborative whiteboarding and explicit dependency graphs for lemmas.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~8–12 hours/week for theory-heavy reading and problem sessions.
mentors.lab_focus: Cole Lab for Discrete Structures & Optimization foregrounds crisp problem formulation and honest theorem statements.

mentors.name: Thomas Brown
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Thomas Brown directs the Brown Lab for Adaptive ML, focusing on representation learning pipelines where scale, compute budgets, and evaluation integrity must be co-designed rather than treated as afterthoughts. Recent technical themes include structured latent variables, representation alignment under distribution shift, and practical tooling for reproducible experimentation. Impact is pursued through open benchmarks, reference implementations, and documentation aimed at lowering the barrier for responsible adoption. Another emphasis is compute-aware research: understanding when scaling helps, when it obscures bugs, and how to prioritize experiments under deadline pressure. Undergraduate researchers typically begin with reproduction and auditing tasks, then graduate to proposing extensions; Dr. Dr. Thomas Brown prioritizes weekly technical checkpoints and written milestone notes. Teaching interfaces include graduate seminars on probabilistic ML and undergraduate courses that foreground debugging culture and scientific communication. Near-term directions include richer evaluation for generative systems, improved protocols for human-in-the-loop supervision, and tooling that makes robust experimentation teachable—not only feasible for experts. The lab tracks emerging ethics guidance from professional societies and updates study protocols when norms evolve. The group actively participates in department reading groups, artifact reviews, and undergraduate research symposia so students experience the social norms of scientific criticism—not only isolated coding tasks.
mentors.preferred_background: Students who can translate vague ideas into measurable hypotheses, document failures, and iterate with statistical discipline.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Thomas Brown has published extensively in top ML venues; the Brown Lab for Adaptive ML routinely releases artifacts and participates in community benchmarking efforts.
mentors.advising_style: Hands-on debugging sessions early in a project, transitioning to student-led ownership after Milestone 1.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~8–12 hours/week for coursework-heavy terms, with flexible scheduling around exams.
mentors.lab_focus: Brown Lab for Adaptive ML balances ambitious modeling ideas with mundane-but-critical validation discipline.

mentors.name: William Russell
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. William Russell directs the Imaging Intelligence Group (Russell) with a focus on 3D scene understanding, integrating learning-based inference with geometric priors when they are trustworthy. The group combines modern representation learning with classical geometry and careful dataset audits; students learn to question shortcuts like overly tidy benchmarks. Applications include assistive technologies, autonomous systems research platforms, and scientific imaging where interpretability and failure visibility matter. Key challenges include domain shift, label noise, and defining realistic evaluation scenarios beyond curated sets. Undergraduates often begin by reproducing baselines on internal datasets, then implement diagnostics (slice analysis, failure clustering); Dr. Dr. William Russell emphasizes milestone demos. Teaching bridges classical vision with contemporary deep learning labs emphasizing debugging and visualization. Near-term directions include robust multimodal perception, efficient training for edge devices, and evaluation tooling for long-tail behaviors. Accessibility is treated as an engineering constraint: interfaces, tutorial videos, and written instructions aim for inclusive defaults rather than retrofits. Funding mixes federal grants, industry partnerships, and internal fellowships; undergraduates may contribute to grant reporting artifacts, milestones, and reproducibility supplements when appropriate. Software stewardship matters: repositories include contribution guidelines, pinned environments, and archived experiment snapshots so future students can continue rather than restart.
mentors.preferred_background: Prior exposure to CV coursework or robotics perception; interest in both modeling and evaluation protocols.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. William Russell publishes in CVPR/ICCV-style venues; Imaging Intelligence Group (Russell) regularly releases datasets and reference training scripts.
mentors.advising_style: Biweekly deep dives plus asynchronous code review; prefers demo-driven progress checks.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~10–14 hours/week depending on dataset construction phases.
mentors.lab_focus: Imaging Intelligence Group (Russell) focuses on perception stacks where visual correctness must be validated, not assumed.

mentors.name: Nadia Cox
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Nadia Cox leads the Cox Interactive Systems Group, designing interactive systems where human workflows—not model scores—define success. Research mixes iterative prototyping with controlled studies and field deployments when feasible. Domains include education technologies, developer tools, healthcare workflows, and civic participation interfaces. Challenges include generalizing findings across populations and avoiding 'demo bait' without measurable outcomes. Students often begin with literature syntheses and pilot studies; Dr. Dr. Nadia Cox coaches storytelling for mixed-methods results. Teaching emphasizes design rationales, ethics, and statistical humility. Directions include human-AI teaming interfaces, accessibility-first AI assistants, and governance tooling for classroom deployments. Outreach includes mentoring novice programmers in adjacent majors when projects demand interdisciplinary teams. Alumni stay loosely connected through shared Slack archives and annual reunions; returning visitors often co-advise capstone threads tied to earlier codebases.
mentors.preferred_background: Comfort translating qualitative insights into design iterations; respects participant consent and privacy.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Nadia Cox publishes in CHI/CSCW/UIST venues; Cox Interactive Systems Group collaborates with education and clinical partners under IRB oversight.
mentors.advising_style: Studio-style critiques plus structured study milestones; encourages reflective design notebooks.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~8–12 hours/week; study seasons may require coordinated scheduling with participants.

mentors.name: Christopher Gonzalez
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Christopher Gonzalez directs the Gonzalez Lab for Distributed & Cloud Systems, building systems where latency tails, resource fairness, and operational observability are engineered—not bolted on after deployment. The group combines kernel/user-space prototyping with rigorous benchmarking and failure injection; students learn to distrust cached conclusions without flamegraphs. Targets include ML training clusters, edge inference stacks, and campus-scale research platforms requiring reliability. Core challenges include tail latency under contention, safe upgrades in distributed settings, and reproducible performance evaluation. Undergraduates often start with instrumented micro-benchmarks and bug fixes; Dr. Dr. Christopher Gonzalez emphasizes incremental commits and design docs. Teaching emphasizes OS internals, concurrency reasoning, and performance culture grounded in measurement. Roadmap themes include systems support for large-model training, energy-aware scheduling, and verified rollback tooling. Accessibility is treated as an engineering constraint: interfaces, tutorial videos, and written instructions aim for inclusive defaults rather than retrofits. When hardware dependencies arise, students learn budgeting basics, cluster etiquette, and carbon-aware experiment planning as part of responsible systems citizenship.
mentors.preferred_background: Students comfortable reading OSDI/SOSP-style papers and annotating design tradeoffs.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Christopher Gonzalez publishes in OSDI/SOSP/NSDI-class venues; Gonzalez Lab for Distributed & Cloud Systems ships artifacts used by partner research groups.
mentors.advising_style: Issue-tracker driven development; expects design notes before large refactors.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~12–18 hours/week during kernel-heavy phases; schedules adjust around compile/debug cycles.
mentors.lab_focus: Gonzalez Lab for Distributed & Cloud Systems treats systems research as principled engineering with scientific measurement obligations.

mentors.name: Daniel Murphy
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Daniel Murphy leads the Murphy Lab for Adaptive ML and studies learning algorithms that remain reliable when data are biased, partially labeled, or collected under shifting conditions. The research program mixes large-scale training insights with smaller diagnostic studies that isolate failure modes in optimization, generalization, or calibration. Application targets span scientific computing assistants, decision-support tools, and safety-critical settings where uncertainty communication matters as much as raw accuracy. The group invests heavily in measurement: defining tasks beyond single-number scores, auditing slices, and studying intervention sensitivity. Undergraduate researchers typically begin with reproduction and auditing tasks, then graduate to proposing extensions; Dr. Dr. Daniel Murphy prioritizes weekly technical checkpoints and written milestone notes. Teaching interfaces include graduate seminars on probabilistic ML and undergraduate courses that foreground debugging culture and scientific communication. Near-term directions include richer evaluation for generative systems, improved protocols for human-in-the-loop supervision, and tooling that makes robust experimentation teachable—not only feasible for experts. Career mentoring includes guidance on graduate school timing, portfolio construction, and translating research experience into interviews—without guaranteeing outcomes. Alumni stay loosely connected through shared Slack archives and annual reunions; returning visitors often co-advise capstone threads tied to earlier codebases. Where feasible, projects publish negative results, ablation catalogs, and limitation sections that later cohorts can build upon without repeating dead ends.
mentors.preferred_background: Prior coursework in ML + algorithms; interest in either theory-backed empirical work or careful systems-aware ML research.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Daniel Murphy advises thesis work spanning theory-informed empirical ML and applied collaborations; alumni have joined both academic labs and industry research teams.
mentors.advising_style: Structured weekly meetings with rolling agendas; prefers shared notebooks and documented experiment plans.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~10–14 focused hours/week during steady semesters; heavier spikes near deadlines are avoided via earlier checkpoints.
mentors.lab_focus: Murphy Lab for Adaptive ML balances ambitious modeling ideas with mundane-but-critical validation discipline.

mentors.name: James Kelly
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. James Kelly leads the Center-scale ML Research Studio (Kelly) and studies learning algorithms that remain reliable when data are biased, partially labeled, or collected under shifting conditions. Methodologically, the group combines modern deep architectures with careful probabilistic framing, stress-testing claims through synthetic controls and targeted interventions rather than leaderboard chasing alone. Application targets span scientific computing assistants, decision-support tools, and safety-critical settings where uncertainty communication matters as much as raw accuracy. A recurring challenge is separating incidental correlations from stable mechanisms—students are encouraged to treat surprising gains as hypotheses that demand mechanism-level scrutiny. Undergraduate researchers typically begin with reproduction and auditing tasks, then graduate to proposing extensions; Dr. Dr. James Kelly prioritizes weekly technical checkpoints and written milestone notes. Course connections emphasize bridging mathematical intuition with hands-on labs that mirror research workflows. Near-term directions include richer evaluation for generative systems, improved protocols for human-in-the-loop supervision, and tooling that makes robust experimentation teachable—not only feasible for experts. Outreach includes mentoring novice programmers in adjacent majors when projects demand interdisciplinary teams. Collaborations extend to neighboring engineering departments, clinics, libraries, and civic partners when projects require domain expertise; students are expected to document assumptions gathered from stakeholders. The lab maintains explicit policies on authorship credit, noting that meaningful intellectual contributions—not  merely routine engineering—guide ordering conversations.
mentors.preferred_background: Students who can translate vague ideas into measurable hypotheses, document failures, and iterate with statistical discipline.

mentors.name: Emily Jenkins
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Emily Jenkins directs the Jenkins Lab for Distributed & Cloud Systems, building systems where latency tails, resource fairness, and operational observability are engineered—not bolted on after deployment. The group combines kernel/user-space prototyping with rigorous benchmarking and failure injection; students learn to distrust cached conclusions without flamegraphs. Targets include ML training clusters, edge inference stacks, and campus-scale research platforms requiring reliability. Core challenges include tail latency under contention, safe upgrades in distributed settings, and reproducible performance evaluation. Undergraduates often start with instrumented micro-benchmarks and bug fixes; Dr. Dr. Emily Jenkins emphasizes incremental commits and design docs. Teaching emphasizes OS internals, concurrency reasoning, and performance culture grounded in measurement. Roadmap themes include systems support for large-model training, energy-aware scheduling, and verified rollback tooling. Outreach includes mentoring novice programmers in adjacent majors when projects demand interdisciplinary teams. International readers are considered default: documentation strives for precise terminology, neutral examples, and careful handling of culturally sensitive applications. Community norms emphasize kindness rigor: critiques target claims and methods, not individuals; disagreement is modeled as a productive part of research meetings.
mentors.preferred_background: Students comfortable reading OSDI/SOSP-style papers and annotating design tradeoffs.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Emily Jenkins publishes in OSDI/SOSP/NSDI-class venues; Jenkins Lab for Distributed & Cloud Systems ships artifacts used by partner research groups.
mentors.advising_style: Issue-tracker driven development; expects design notes before large refactors.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~12–18 hours/week during kernel-heavy phases; schedules adjust around compile/debug cycles.
mentors.lab_focus: Jenkins Lab for Distributed & Cloud Systems treats systems research as principled engineering with scientific measurement obligations.

mentors.name: Joseph Foster
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Joseph Foster directs the Foster Security & Privacy Lab, studying defenses that survive motivated adversaries rather than nominal benchmarks. Methods combine empirical attack surfaces with principled mitigations and measurable residual risk articulation. Applications span campus infrastructure hardening, privacy-preserving analytics, and secure ML deployment pipelines. Key tensions include usability vs. assurance, patch latency vs. verification, and evaluating defenses under adaptive attackers. Undergraduates begin with reproduction of CVE analyses and controlled experiments; Dr. Dr. Joseph Foster emphasizes ethics training. Courses integrate threat modeling exercises with hands-on labs in a supervised environment. Roadmap directions include supply-chain transparency tooling, privacy for graph analytics, and robustness of ML systems under prompt injection. Software stewardship matters: repositories include contribution guidelines, pinned environments, and archived experiment snapshots so future students can continue rather than restart. Where feasible, projects publish negative results, ablation catalogs, and limitation sections that later cohorts can build upon without repeating dead ends.
mentors.preferred_background: Security mindset: assumes attackers exploit ambiguity; expects precise threat models and cautious claims.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Joseph Foster publishes in IEEE S&P/CCS/USENIX Security circles; Foster Security & Privacy Lab collaborates with systems and policy researchers.
mentors.advising_style: Structured threat-model reviews; expects careful logging of assumptions and ethical boundaries.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~10–14 hours/week; lab work may include supervised penetration-testing exercises on isolated testbeds.
mentors.lab_focus: Foster Security & Privacy Lab treats security research as adversarial measurement science.

mentors.name: Anna Young
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Anna Young directs the Imaging Intelligence Group (Young) with a focus on 3D scene understanding, integrating learning-based inference with geometric priors when they are trustworthy. The group combines modern representation learning with classical geometry and careful dataset audits; students learn to question shortcuts like overly tidy benchmarks. Applications include assistive technologies, autonomous systems research platforms, and scientific imaging where interpretability and failure visibility matter. Key challenges include domain shift, label noise, and defining realistic evaluation scenarios beyond curated sets. Undergraduates often begin by reproducing baselines on internal datasets, then implement diagnostics (slice analysis, failure clustering); Dr. Dr. Anna Young emphasizes milestone demos. Teaching bridges classical vision with contemporary deep learning labs emphasizing debugging and visualization. Near-term directions include robust multimodal perception, efficient training for edge devices, and evaluation tooling for long-tail behaviors. Outreach includes mentoring novice programmers in adjacent majors when projects demand interdisciplinary teams. Where feasible, projects publish negative results, ablation catalogs, and limitation sections that later cohorts can build upon without repeating dead ends.

mentors.advising_style: Biweekly deep dives plus asynchronous code review; prefers demo-driven progress checks.

mentors.name: Zara Kim
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Zara Kim directs the Kim Systems Security Group, studying defenses that survive motivated adversaries rather than nominal benchmarks. Methods combine empirical attack surfaces with principled mitigations and measurable residual risk articulation. Applications span campus infrastructure hardening, privacy-preserving analytics, and secure ML deployment pipelines. Key tensions include usability vs. assurance, patch latency vs. verification, and evaluating defenses under adaptive attackers. Undergraduates begin with reproduction of CVE analyses and controlled experiments; Dr. Dr. Zara Kim emphasizes ethics training. Courses integrate threat modeling exercises with hands-on labs in a supervised environment. Roadmap directions include supply-chain transparency tooling, privacy for graph analytics, and robustness of ML systems under prompt injection. Community norms emphasize kindness rigor: critiques target claims and methods, not individuals; disagreement is modeled as a productive part of research meetings. Where feasible, projects publish negative results, ablation catalogs, and limitation sections that later cohorts can build upon without repeating dead ends. Career mentoring includes guidance on graduate school timing, portfolio construction, and translating research experience into interviews—without guaranteeing outcomes.
mentors.preferred_background: Security mindset: assumes attackers exploit ambiguity; expects precise threat models and cautious claims.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Zara Kim publishes in IEEE S&P/CCS/USENIX Security circles; Kim Systems Security Group collaborates with systems and policy researchers.
mentors.advising_style: Structured threat-model reviews; expects careful logging of assumptions and ethical boundaries.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~10–14 hours/week; lab work may include supervised penetration-testing exercises on isolated testbeds.

mentors.name: Naomi Cox
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Naomi Cox directs the Cox High-Performance Computing Group, building systems where latency tails, resource fairness, and operational observability are engineered—not bolted on after deployment. The group combines kernel/user-space prototyping with rigorous benchmarking and failure injection; students learn to distrust cached conclusions without flamegraphs. Targets include ML training clusters, edge inference stacks, and campus-scale research platforms requiring reliability. Core challenges include tail latency under contention, safe upgrades in distributed settings, and reproducible performance evaluation. Undergraduates often start with instrumented micro-benchmarks and bug fixes; Dr. Dr. Naomi Cox emphasizes incremental commits and design docs. Teaching emphasizes OS internals, concurrency reasoning, and performance culture grounded in measurement. Roadmap themes include systems support for large-model training, energy-aware scheduling, and verified rollback tooling. The lab maintains explicit policies on authorship credit, noting that meaningful intellectual contributions—not  merely routine engineering—guide ordering conversations. Accessibility is treated as an engineering constraint: interfaces, tutorial videos, and written instructions aim for inclusive defaults rather than retrofits.
mentors.preferred_background: Strong systems programming maturity and patience for long debugging cycles; coursework in OS + networking helps substantially.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Naomi Cox publishes in OSDI/SOSP/NSDI-class venues; Cox High-Performance Computing Group ships artifacts used by partner research groups.
mentors.advising_style: Issue-tracker driven development; expects design notes before large refactors.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~12–18 hours/week during kernel-heavy phases; schedules adjust around compile/debug cycles.
mentors.lab_focus: Cox High-Performance Computing Group treats systems research as principled engineering with scientific measurement obligations.

mentors.name: Chloe Cooper
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Chloe Cooper runs the Cooper Lab for Adaptive ML with an emphasis on empirical ML that still respects identifiability constraints, proper ablations, and honest reporting of negative results. Recent technical themes include structured latent variables, representation alignment under distribution shift, and practical tooling for reproducible experimentation. Impact is pursued through open benchmarks, reference implementations, and documentation aimed at lowering the barrier for responsible adoption. A recurring challenge is separating incidental correlations from stable mechanisms—students are encouraged to treat surprising gains as hypotheses that demand mechanism-level scrutiny. Mentorship emphasizes readable code, experiment manifests, and crisp writeups—skills that transfer regardless of whether a project ends in a publication or a strong technical report. Course connections emphasize bridging mathematical intuition with hands-on labs that mirror research workflows. Near-term directions include richer evaluation for generative systems, improved protocols for human-in-the-loop supervision, and tooling that makes robust experimentation teachable—not only feasible for experts. The group actively participates in department reading groups, artifact reviews, and undergraduate research symposia so students experience the social norms of scientific criticism—not only isolated coding tasks. International readers are considered default: documentation strives for precise terminology, neutral examples, and careful handling of culturally sensitive applications. The lab tracks emerging ethics guidance from professional societies and updates study protocols when norms evolve.
mentors.preferred_background: Strong programming maturity and careful experimental habits; comfortable reading NeurIPS/ICML-style papers and reproducing baselines.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Chloe Cooper advises thesis work spanning theory-informed empirical ML and applied collaborations; alumni have joined both academic labs and industry research teams.
mentors.advising_style: Hands-on debugging sessions early in a project, transitioning to student-led ownership after Milestone 1.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~8–12 hours/week for coursework-heavy terms, with flexible scheduling around exams.
mentors.lab_focus: Cooper Lab for Adaptive ML balances ambitious modeling ideas with mundane-but-critical validation discipline.

mentors.name: Sofia Long
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Sofia Long leads the Long Human-Centered Computing Lab, designing interactive systems where human workflows—not model scores—define success. Research mixes iterative prototyping with controlled studies and field deployments when feasible. Domains include education technologies, developer tools, healthcare workflows, and civic participation interfaces. Challenges include generalizing findings across populations and avoiding 'demo bait' without measurable outcomes. Students often begin with literature syntheses and pilot studies; Dr. Dr. Sofia Long coaches storytelling for mixed-methods results. Teaching emphasizes design rationales, ethics, and statistical humility. Directions include human-AI teaming interfaces, accessibility-first AI assistants, and governance tooling for classroom deployments. Software stewardship matters: repositories include contribution guidelines, pinned environments, and archived experiment snapshots so future students can continue rather than restart. The lab tracks emerging ethics guidance from professional societies and updates study protocols when norms evolve.
mentors.preferred_background: Interest in combining lightweight engineering with rigorous study protocols.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Sofia Long publishes in CHI/CSCW/UIST venues; Long Human-Centered Computing Lab collaborates with education and clinical partners under IRB oversight.

mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~8–12 hours/week; study seasons may require coordinated scheduling with participants.

mentors.name: Anna Ramirez
mentors.department: Department of Electrical Engineering & Computer Science
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Anna Ramirez directs the Ramirez Systems Security Group, studying defenses that survive motivated adversaries rather than nominal benchmarks. Methods combine empirical attack surfaces with principled mitigations and measurable residual risk articulation. Applications span campus infrastructure hardening, privacy-preserving analytics, and secure ML deployment pipelines. Key tensions include usability vs. assurance, patch latency vs. verification, and evaluating defenses under adaptive attackers. Undergraduates begin with reproduction of CVE analyses and controlled experiments; Dr. Dr. Anna Ramirez emphasizes ethics training. Courses integrate threat modeling exercises with hands-on labs in a supervised environment. Roadmap directions include supply-chain transparency tooling, privacy for graph analytics, and robustness of ML systems under prompt injection. When hardware dependencies arise, students learn budgeting basics, cluster etiquette, and carbon-aware experiment planning as part of responsible systems citizenship. International readers are considered default: documentation strives for precise terminology, neutral examples, and careful handling of culturally sensitive applications.
mentors.preferred_background: Prior coursework in security or systems; patience for responsible disclosure norms.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Anna Ramirez publishes in IEEE S&P/CCS/USENIX Security circles; Ramirez Systems Security Group collaborates with systems and policy researchers.
mentors.advising_style: Structured threat-model reviews; expects careful logging of assumptions and ethical boundaries.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~10–14 hours/week; lab work may include supervised penetration-testing exercises on isolated testbeds.

mentors.name: Jordan Simmons
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Jordan Simmons runs the Laboratory for Reliable Learning with an emphasis on empirical ML that still respects identifiability constraints, proper ablations, and honest reporting of negative results. The research program mixes large-scale training insights with smaller diagnostic studies that isolate failure modes in optimization, generalization, or calibration. Application targets span scientific computing assistants, decision-support tools, and safety-critical settings where uncertainty communication matters as much as raw accuracy. A recurring challenge is separating incidental correlations from stable mechanisms—students are encouraged to treat surprising gains as hypotheses that demand mechanism-level scrutiny. Undergraduate researchers typically begin with reproduction and auditing tasks, then graduate to proposing extensions; Dr. Dr. Jordan Simmons prioritizes weekly technical checkpoints and written milestone notes. Teaching interfaces include graduate seminars on probabilistic ML and undergraduate courses that foreground debugging culture and scientific communication. Near-term directions include richer evaluation for generative systems, improved protocols for human-in-the-loop supervision, and tooling that makes robust experimentation teachable—not only feasible for experts. Accessibility is treated as an engineering constraint: interfaces, tutorial videos, and written instructions aim for inclusive defaults rather than retrofits. International readers are considered default: documentation strives for precise terminology, neutral examples, and careful handling of culturally sensitive applications.
mentors.preferred_background: Prior coursework in ML + algorithms; interest in either theory-backed empirical work or careful systems-aware ML research.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Jordan Simmons has published extensively in top ML venues; the Laboratory for Reliable Learning routinely releases artifacts and participates in community benchmarking efforts.
mentors.advising_style: Structured weekly meetings with rolling agendas; prefers shared notebooks and documented experiment plans.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~8–12 hours/week for coursework-heavy terms, with flexible scheduling around exams.
mentors.lab_focus: Laboratory for Reliable Learning emphasizes reproducible ML research with measurable claims and collaborative tooling.
