mentors.name: Jordan Torres
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Jordan Torres leads the Torres Algorithms & Theory Group, investigating fundamental computation limits and designing algorithms with provable guarantees under realistic constraints. Research blends classical discrete optimization with modern probabilistic tools and structured instance analyses. Motivating domains include routing, scheduling, learning-adjacent subroutines, and resource allocation where constants and logarithmic factors genuinely matter. Students confront tension between worst-case guarantees and empirical structure—projects clarify assumptions explicitly. Mentorship emphasizes lemma notebooks, proof sketches before details, and seminar-style presentations; Dr. Dr. Jordan Torres coaches undergraduates toward incremental publishable lemmas when appropriate. Teaching stresses mathematical communication and proof hygiene alongside algorithm design patterns. Directions include dynamic graph algorithms under partial observability, approximation schemes for structured clustering, and bridges to ML theory. The lab maintains explicit policies on authorship credit, noting that meaningful intellectual contributions—not  merely routine engineering—guide ordering conversations. Software stewardship matters: repositories include contribution guidelines, pinned environments, and archived experiment snapshots so future students can continue rather than restart. The group actively participates in department reading groups, artifact reviews, and undergraduate research symposia so students experience the social norms of scientific criticism—not only isolated coding tasks.
mentors.preferred_background: Strong performance in algorithms/theory coursework and willingness to read classical references.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Jordan Torres publishes in STOC/FOCS/SODA-class venues; Torres Algorithms & Theory Group welcomes careful undergraduate collaborators on theory-meets-systems bridges.
mentors.advising_style: Weekly proof meetings with collaborative whiteboarding and explicit dependency graphs for lemmas.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~8–12 hours/week for theory-heavy reading and problem sessions.
mentors.lab_focus: Torres Algorithms & Theory Group foregrounds crisp problem formulation and honest theorem statements.

Eric Chavez
Department of Computing
Research areas: performance engineering; cloud infrastructure; storage systems; operating systems
Keywords: microservices; NVMe; Rust; C++; Linux kernel; RDMA; Kubernetes
Dr. Dr. Eric Chavez directs the Chavez Lab for Distributed & Cloud Systems, building systems where latency tails, resource fairness, and operational observability are engineered—not bolted on after deployment. The group combines kernel/user-space prototyping with rigorous benchmarking and failure injection; students learn to distrust cached conclusions without flamegraphs. Targets include ML training clusters, edge inference stacks, and campus-scale research platforms requiring reliability. Core challenges include tail latency under contention, safe upgrades in distributed settings, and reproducible performance evaluation. Undergraduates often start with instrumented micro-benchmarks and bug fixes; Dr. Dr. Eric Chavez emphasizes incremental commits and design docs. Teaching emphasizes OS internals, concurrency reasoning, and performance culture grounded in measurement. Roadmap themes include systems support for large-model training, energy-aware scheduling, and verified rollback tooling. Software stewardship matters: repositories include contribution guidelines, pinned environments, and archived experiment snapshots so future students can continue rather than restart. International readers are considered default: documentation strives for precise terminology, neutral examples, and careful handling of culturally sensitive applications.

mentors.name: Zara Gonzalez
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Zara Gonzalez leads the Gonzalez Lab for Discrete Structures & Optimization, investigating fundamental computation limits and designing algorithms with provable guarantees under realistic constraints. Research blends classical discrete optimization with modern probabilistic tools and structured instance analyses. Motivating domains include routing, scheduling, learning-adjacent subroutines, and resource allocation where constants and logarithmic factors genuinely matter. Students confront tension between worst-case guarantees and empirical structure—projects clarify assumptions explicitly. Mentorship emphasizes lemma notebooks, proof sketches before details, and seminar-style presentations; Dr. Dr. Zara Gonzalez coaches undergraduates toward incremental publishable lemmas when appropriate. Teaching stresses mathematical communication and proof hygiene alongside algorithm design patterns. Directions include dynamic graph algorithms under partial observability, approximation schemes for structured clustering, and bridges to ML theory. Funding mixes federal grants, industry partnerships, and internal fellowships; undergraduates may contribute to grant reporting artifacts, milestones, and reproducibility supplements when appropriate. Collaborations extend to neighboring engineering departments, clinics, libraries, and civic partners when projects require domain expertise; students are expected to document assumptions gathered from stakeholders.
mentors.preferred_background: Strong performance in algorithms/theory coursework and willingness to read classical references.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Zara Gonzalez publishes in STOC/FOCS/SODA-class venues; Gonzalez Lab for Discrete Structures & Optimization welcomes careful undergraduate collaborators on theory-meets-systems bridges.
mentors.advising_style: Weekly proof meetings with collaborative whiteboarding and explicit dependency graphs for lemmas.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~8–12 hours/week for theory-heavy reading and problem sessions.

mentors.name: Maria Wright
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Maria Wright runs the Wright Lab for Adaptive ML with an emphasis on empirical ML that still respects identifiability constraints, proper ablations, and honest reporting of negative results. The research program mixes large-scale training insights with smaller diagnostic studies that isolate failure modes in optimization, generalization, or calibration. The lab collaborates with campus partners on climate modeling workflows, biomedical signal pipelines, and education technologies where data governance is non-negotiable. Another emphasis is compute-aware research: understanding when scaling helps, when it obscures bugs, and how to prioritize experiments under deadline pressure. Undergraduate researchers typically begin with reproduction and auditing tasks, then graduate to proposing extensions; Dr. Dr. Maria Wright prioritizes weekly technical checkpoints and written milestone notes. Course connections emphasize bridging mathematical intuition with hands-on labs that mirror research workflows. Students interested in interdisciplinary projects should expect to spend time understanding domain constraints before proposing model changes. Where feasible, projects publish negative results, ablation catalogs, and limitation sections that later cohorts can build upon without repeating dead ends. Collaborations extend to neighboring engineering departments, clinics, libraries, and civic partners when projects require domain expertise; students are expected to document assumptions gathered from stakeholders.
mentors.preferred_background: Prior coursework in ML + algorithms; interest in either theory-backed empirical work or careful systems-aware ML research.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Maria Wright advises thesis work spanning theory-informed empirical ML and applied collaborations; alumni have joined both academic labs and industry research teams.
mentors.advising_style: Hands-on debugging sessions early in a project, transitioning to student-led ownership after Milestone 1.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~10–14 focused hours/week during steady semesters; heavier spikes near deadlines are avoided via earlier checkpoints.
mentors.lab_focus: Wright Lab for Adaptive ML balances ambitious modeling ideas with mundane-but-critical validation discipline.

mentors.name: Rosa Allen
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Rosa Allen leads the Allen Lab for Grounded NLP, focusing on language technologies where grounding, attribution, and evaluation honesty are first-class requirements—not optional polish. Research mixes transformer-era modeling with careful dataset governance, human protocols, and targeted stress tests for brittleness. Applications include decision-support assistants, scientific literature workflows, and accessibility tools with explicit uncertainty handling. Open challenges include contamination-aware benchmarking, multilingual fairness, and scaling supervision without amplifying silent biases. Students typically begin with reproduction and error analysis, then move to benchmark proposals; Dr. Dr. Rosa Allen emphasizes writing-as-thinking. Courses emphasize NLP evaluation literacy and the sociology of dataset creation. Roadmap themes include retrieval-first architectures, structured reasoning interfaces, and lightweight auditing tools for classroom deployments. The lab tracks emerging ethics guidance from professional societies and updates study protocols when norms evolve. The lab maintains explicit policies on authorship credit, noting that meaningful intellectual contributions—not  merely routine engineering—guide ordering conversations.
mentors.preferred_background: Comfortable reading ACL-style papers; cares about evaluation design and responsible deployment narratives.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Rosa Allen publishes across ACL/EMNLP/NAACL venues; Allen Lab for Grounded NLP collaborates with HCI and policy scholars on deployment studies.
mentors.advising_style: Paper-style milestone memos; prefers annotated bibliographies early in a project.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~9–13 hours/week with heavier reading loads early on.
mentors.lab_focus: Allen Lab for Grounded NLP treats language models as systems requiring measurement programs, not single-score comparisons.

mentors.name: Ryan Nelson
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Ryan Nelson directs the Nelson Vision & Perception Lab with a focus on 3D scene understanding, integrating learning-based inference with geometric priors when they are trustworthy. Method work spans efficient architectures, training curricula, and post-training calibration for deployed stacks. Applications include assistive technologies, autonomous systems research platforms, and scientific imaging where interpretability and failure visibility matter. Key challenges include domain shift, label noise, and defining realistic evaluation scenarios beyond curated sets. Undergraduates often begin by reproducing baselines on internal datasets, then implement diagnostics (slice analysis, failure clustering); Dr. Dr. Ryan Nelson emphasizes milestone demos. Teaching bridges classical vision with contemporary deep learning labs emphasizing debugging and visualization. Near-term directions include robust multimodal perception, efficient training for edge devices, and evaluation tooling for long-tail behaviors. Students are encouraged to present at internal seminars before external deadlines, practicing clarity under questions from faculty outside their immediate specialty. Collaborations extend to neighboring engineering departments, clinics, libraries, and civic partners when projects require domain expertise; students are expected to document assumptions gathered from stakeholders.
mentors.preferred_background: Strong linear algebra and comfort with probability; willingness to build data pipelines and sanity-check visual outputs.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Ryan Nelson publishes in CVPR/ICCV-style venues; Nelson Vision & Perception Lab regularly releases datasets and reference training scripts.
mentors.advising_style: Biweekly deep dives plus asynchronous code review; prefers demo-driven progress checks.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~10–14 hours/week depending on dataset construction phases.
mentors.lab_focus: Nelson Vision & Perception Lab focuses on perception stacks where visual correctness must be validated, not assumed.

Research mixes transformer-era modeling with careful dataset governance, human protocols, and targeted stress tests for brittleness. Applications include decision-support assistants, scientific literature workflows, and accessibility tools with explicit uncertainty handling. Open challenges include contamination-aware benchmarking, multilingual fairness, and scaling supervision without amplifying silent biases. Students typically begin with reproduction and error analysis, then move to benchmark proposals; Dr. Dr. Rachel Singh emphasizes writing-as-thinking. Courses emphasize NLP evaluation literacy and the sociology of dataset creation. Roadmap themes include retrieval-first architectures, structured reasoning interfaces, and lightweight auditing tools for classroom deployments. Students are encouraged to present at internal seminars before external deadlines, practicing clarity under questions from faculty outside their immediate specialty. When hardware dependencies arise, students learn budgeting basics, cluster etiquette, and carbon-aware experiment planning as part of responsible systems citizenship.

mentors.name: Amelia Baker
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Amelia Baker leads the Baker Vision & Perception Lab, developing perception models that remain dependable across lighting, sensor noise, and deployment constraints common in mobile and robotics settings. Method work spans efficient architectures, training curricula, and post-training calibration for deployed stacks. Applications include assistive technologies, autonomous systems research platforms, and scientific imaging where interpretability and failure visibility matter. Key challenges include domain shift, label noise, and defining realistic evaluation scenarios beyond curated sets. Undergraduates often begin by reproducing baselines on internal datasets, then implement diagnostics (slice analysis, failure clustering); Dr. Dr. Amelia Baker emphasizes milestone demos. Teaching bridges classical vision with contemporary deep learning labs emphasizing debugging and visualization. Near-term directions include robust multimodal perception, efficient training for edge devices, and evaluation tooling for long-tail behaviors. The lab tracks emerging ethics guidance from professional societies and updates study protocols when norms evolve. Career mentoring includes guidance on graduate school timing, portfolio construction, and translating research experience into interviews—without guaranteeing outcomes. Funding mixes federal grants, industry partnerships, and internal fellowships; undergraduates may contribute to grant reporting artifacts, milestones, and reproducibility supplements when appropriate.
mentors.preferred_background: Prior exposure to CV coursework or robotics perception; interest in both modeling and evaluation protocols.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Amelia Baker publishes in CVPR/ICCV-style venues; Baker Vision & Perception Lab regularly releases datasets and reference training scripts.
mentors.advising_style: Biweekly deep dives plus asynchronous code review; prefers demo-driven progress checks.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~10–14 hours/week depending on dataset construction phases.
mentors.lab_focus: Baker Vision & Perception Lab focuses on perception stacks where visual correctness must be validated, not assumed.

mentors.name: Brooke James
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Brooke James runs the Laboratory for Reliable Learning with an emphasis on empirical ML that still respects identifiability constraints, proper ablations, and honest reporting of negative results. Recent technical themes include structured latent variables, representation alignment under distribution shift, and practical tooling for reproducible experimentation. The lab collaborates with campus partners on climate modeling workflows, biomedical signal pipelines, and education technologies where data governance is non-negotiable. The group invests heavily in measurement: defining tasks beyond single-number scores, auditing slices, and studying intervention sensitivity. Mentorship emphasizes readable code, experiment manifests, and crisp writeups—skills that transfer regardless of whether a project ends in a publication or a strong technical report. Course connections emphasize bridging mathematical intuition with hands-on labs that mirror research workflows. Students interested in interdisciplinary projects should expect to spend time understanding domain constraints before proposing model changes. When hardware dependencies arise, students learn budgeting basics, cluster etiquette, and carbon-aware experiment planning as part of responsible systems citizenship. Accessibility is treated as an engineering constraint: interfaces, tutorial videos, and written instructions aim for inclusive defaults rather than retrofits. Students are encouraged to present at internal seminars before external deadlines, practicing clarity under questions from faculty outside their immediate specialty.
mentors.preferred_background: Prior coursework in ML + algorithms; interest in either theory-backed empirical work or careful systems-aware ML research.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Brooke James has published extensively in top ML venues; the Laboratory for Reliable Learning routinely releases artifacts and participates in community benchmarking efforts.
mentors.advising_style: Structured weekly meetings with rolling agendas; prefers shared notebooks and documented experiment plans.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~8–12 hours/week for coursework-heavy terms, with flexible scheduling around exams.
mentors.lab_focus: Laboratory for Reliable Learning emphasizes reproducible ML research with measurable claims and collaborative tooling.

mentors.name: Justin Chen
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Justin Chen directs the Chen High-Performance Computing Group, building systems where latency tails, resource fairness, and operational observability are engineered—not bolted on after deployment. The group combines kernel/user-space prototyping with rigorous benchmarking and failure injection; students learn to distrust cached conclusions without flamegraphs. Targets include ML training clusters, edge inference stacks, and campus-scale research platforms requiring reliability. Core challenges include tail latency under contention, safe upgrades in distributed settings, and reproducible performance evaluation. Undergraduates often start with instrumented micro-benchmarks and bug fixes; Dr. Dr. Justin Chen emphasizes incremental commits and design docs. Teaching emphasizes OS internals, concurrency reasoning, and performance culture grounded in measurement. Roadmap themes include systems support for large-model training, energy-aware scheduling, and verified rollback tooling. International readers are considered default: documentation strives for precise terminology, neutral examples, and careful handling of culturally sensitive applications. The lab tracks emerging ethics guidance from professional societies and updates study protocols when norms evolve. Software stewardship matters: repositories include contribution guidelines, pinned environments, and archived experiment snapshots so future students can continue rather than restart.
mentors.preferred_background: Strong systems programming maturity and patience for long debugging cycles; coursework in OS + networking helps substantially.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Justin Chen publishes in OSDI/SOSP/NSDI-class venues; Chen High-Performance Computing Group ships artifacts used by partner research groups.
mentors.advising_style: Issue-tracker driven development; expects design notes before large refactors.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~12–18 hours/week during kernel-heavy phases; schedules adjust around compile/debug cycles.
mentors.lab_focus: Chen High-Performance Computing Group treats systems research as principled engineering with scientific measurement obligations.

mentors.name: Keiko Sanders
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Keiko Sanders leads the Sanders Human-Centered Computing Lab, designing interactive systems where human workflows—not model scores—define success. Research mixes iterative prototyping with controlled studies and field deployments when feasible. Domains include education technologies, developer tools, healthcare workflows, and civic participation interfaces. Challenges include generalizing findings across populations and avoiding 'demo bait' without measurable outcomes. Students often begin with literature syntheses and pilot studies; Dr. Dr. Keiko Sanders coaches storytelling for mixed-methods results. Teaching emphasizes design rationales, ethics, and statistical humility. Directions include human-AI teaming interfaces, accessibility-first AI assistants, and governance tooling for classroom deployments. The lab maintains explicit policies on authorship credit, noting that meaningful intellectual contributions—not  merely routine engineering—guide ordering conversations. When hardware dependencies arise, students learn budgeting basics, cluster etiquette, and carbon-aware experiment planning as part of responsible systems citizenship.
mentors.preferred_background: Interest in combining lightweight engineering with rigorous study protocols.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Keiko Sanders publishes in CHI/CSCW/UIST venues; Sanders Human-Centered Computing Lab collaborates with education and clinical partners under IRB oversight.
mentors.advising_style: Studio-style critiques plus structured study milestones; encourages reflective design notebooks.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~8–12 hours/week; study seasons may require coordinated scheduling with participants.
mentors.lab_focus: Sanders Human-Centered Computing Lab emphasizes accountable UX research grounded in participant-centered evidence.

mentors.name: Henry Anderson
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Henry Anderson leads the Anderson Algorithms & Theory Group, investigating fundamental computation limits and designing algorithms with provable guarantees under realistic constraints. Research blends classical discrete optimization with modern probabilistic tools and structured instance analyses. Motivating domains include routing, scheduling, learning-adjacent subroutines, and resource allocation where constants and logarithmic factors genuinely matter. Students confront tension between worst-case guarantees and empirical structure—projects clarify assumptions explicitly. Mentorship emphasizes lemma notebooks, proof sketches before details, and seminar-style presentations; Dr. Dr. Henry Anderson coaches undergraduates toward incremental publishable lemmas when appropriate. Teaching stresses mathematical communication and proof hygiene alongside algorithm design patterns. Directions include dynamic graph algorithms under partial observability, approximation schemes for structured clustering, and bridges to ML theory. The group actively participates in department reading groups, artifact reviews, and undergraduate research symposia so students experience the social norms of scientific criticism—not only isolated coding tasks. Community norms emphasize kindness rigor: critiques target claims and methods, not individuals; disagreement is modeled as a productive part of research meetings. Software stewardship matters: repositories include contribution guidelines, pinned environments, and archived experiment snapshots so future students can continue rather than restart.
mentors.preferred_background: Comfortable with rigorous proofs and iterative refinement of definitions; expects clarity over speed.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Henry Anderson publishes in STOC/FOCS/SODA-class venues; Anderson Algorithms & Theory Group welcomes careful undergraduate collaborators on theory-meets-systems bridges.
mentors.advising_style: Weekly proof meetings with collaborative whiteboarding and explicit dependency graphs for lemmas.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~8–12 hours/week for theory-heavy reading and problem sessions.
mentors.lab_focus: Anderson Algorithms & Theory Group foregrounds crisp problem formulation and honest theorem statements.

mentors.name: Noah Nguyen
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Noah Nguyen leads the Center-scale ML Research Studio (Nguyen) and studies learning algorithms that remain reliable when data are biased, partially labeled, or collected under shifting conditions. The research program mixes large-scale training insights with smaller diagnostic studies that isolate failure modes in optimization, generalization, or calibration. Impact is pursued through open benchmarks, reference implementations, and documentation aimed at lowering the barrier for responsible adoption. Another emphasis is compute-aware research: understanding when scaling helps, when it obscures bugs, and how to prioritize experiments under deadline pressure. Undergraduate researchers typically begin with reproduction and auditing tasks, then graduate to proposing extensions; Dr. Dr. Noah Nguyen prioritizes weekly technical checkpoints and written milestone notes. Course connections emphasize bridging mathematical intuition with hands-on labs that mirror research workflows. Students interested in interdisciplinary projects should expect to spend time understanding domain constraints before proposing model changes. Funding mixes federal grants, industry partnerships, and internal fellowships; undergraduates may contribute to grant reporting artifacts, milestones, and reproducibility supplements when appropriate. Software stewardship matters: repositories include contribution guidelines, pinned environments, and archived experiment snapshots so future students can continue rather than restart.
mentors.preferred_background: Students who can translate vague ideas into measurable hypotheses, document failures, and iterate with statistical discipline.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Noah Nguyen advises thesis work spanning theory-informed empirical ML and applied collaborations; alumni have joined both academic labs and industry research teams.
mentors.advising_style: Hands-on debugging sessions early in a project, transitioning to student-led ownership after Milestone 1.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~10–14 focused hours/week during steady semesters; heavier spikes near deadlines are avoided via earlier checkpoints.
mentors.lab_focus: Center-scale ML Research Studio (Nguyen) balances ambitious modeling ideas with mundane-but-critical validation discipline.

Dr. Dr. Noah Nguyen leads the Center-scale ML Research Studio (Nguyen) and studies learning algorithms that remain reliable when data are biased, partially labeled, or collected under shifting conditions. The research program mixes large-scale training insights with smaller diagnostic studies that isolate failure modes in optimization, generalization, or calibration. Impact is pursued through open benchmarks, reference implementations, and documentation aimed at lowering the barrier for responsible adoption. Another emphasis is compute-aware research: understanding when scaling helps, when it obscures bugs, and how to prioritize experiments under deadline pressure. Undergraduate researchers typically begin with reproduction and auditing tasks, then graduate to proposing extensions; Dr. Dr. Noah Nguyen prioritizes weekly technical checkpoints and written milestone notes. Course connections emphasize bridging mathematical intuition with hands-on labs that mirror research workflows. Students interested in interdisciplinary projects should expect to spend time understanding domain constraints before proposing model changes. Funding mixes federal grants, industry partnerships, and internal fellowships; undergraduates may contribute to grant reporting artifacts, milestones, and reproducibility supplements when appropriate. Software stewardship matters: repositories include contribution guidelines, pinned environments, and archived experiment snapshots so future students can continue rather than restart.

mentors.name: Sofia Wright
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Sofia Wright directs the Wright Security & Privacy Lab, studying defenses that survive motivated adversaries rather than nominal benchmarks. Methods combine empirical attack surfaces with principled mitigations and measurable residual risk articulation. Applications span campus infrastructure hardening, privacy-preserving analytics, and secure ML deployment pipelines. Key tensions include usability vs. assurance, patch latency vs. verification, and evaluating defenses under adaptive attackers. Undergraduates begin with reproduction of CVE analyses and controlled experiments; Dr. Dr. Sofia Wright emphasizes ethics training. Courses integrate threat modeling exercises with hands-on labs in a supervised environment. Roadmap directions include supply-chain transparency tooling, privacy for graph analytics, and robustness of ML systems under prompt injection. Alumni stay loosely connected through shared Slack archives and annual reunions; returning visitors often co-advise capstone threads tied to earlier codebases. Career mentoring includes guidance on graduate school timing, portfolio construction, and translating research experience into interviews—without guaranteeing outcomes. Community norms emphasize kindness rigor: critiques target claims and methods, not individuals; disagreement is modeled as a productive part of research meetings.
mentors.preferred_background: Security mindset: assumes attackers exploit ambiguity; expects precise threat models and cautious claims.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Sofia Wright publishes in IEEE S&P/CCS/USENIX Security circles; Wright Security & Privacy Lab collaborates with systems and policy researchers.
mentors.advising_style: Structured threat-model reviews; expects careful logging of assumptions and ethical boundaries.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~10–14 hours/week; lab work may include supervised penetration-testing exercises on isolated testbeds.

mentors.name: Angela Watson
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Angela Watson leads the Watson Lab for Discrete Structures & Optimization, investigating fundamental computation limits and designing algorithms with provable guarantees under realistic constraints. Research blends classical discrete optimization with modern probabilistic tools and structured instance analyses. Motivating domains include routing, scheduling, learning-adjacent subroutines, and resource allocation where constants and logarithmic factors genuinely matter. Students confront tension between worst-case guarantees and empirical structure—projects clarify assumptions explicitly. Mentorship emphasizes lemma notebooks, proof sketches before details, and seminar-style presentations; Dr. Dr. Angela Watson coaches undergraduates toward incremental publishable lemmas when appropriate. Teaching stresses mathematical communication and proof hygiene alongside algorithm design patterns. Directions include dynamic graph algorithms under partial observability, approximation schemes for structured clustering, and bridges to ML theory. Career mentoring includes guidance on graduate school timing, portfolio construction, and translating research experience into interviews—without guaranteeing outcomes. Software stewardship matters: repositories include contribution guidelines, pinned environments, and archived experiment snapshots so future students can continue rather than restart.

mentors.advising_style: Weekly proof meetings with collaborative whiteboarding and explicit dependency graphs for lemmas.

mentors.name: Zara Price
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Zara Price directs the Price Lab for 3D Understanding with a focus on 3D scene understanding, integrating learning-based inference with geometric priors when they are trustworthy. Method work spans efficient architectures, training curricula, and post-training calibration for deployed stacks. Applications include assistive technologies, autonomous systems research platforms, and scientific imaging where interpretability and failure visibility matter. Key challenges include domain shift, label noise, and defining realistic evaluation scenarios beyond curated sets. Undergraduates often begin by reproducing baselines on internal datasets, then implement diagnostics (slice analysis, failure clustering); Dr. Dr. Zara Price emphasizes milestone demos. Teaching bridges classical vision with contemporary deep learning labs emphasizing debugging and visualization. Near-term directions include robust multimodal perception, efficient training for edge devices, and evaluation tooling for long-tail behaviors. Students are encouraged to present at internal seminars before external deadlines, practicing clarity under questions from faculty outside their immediate specialty. Career mentoring includes guidance on graduate school timing, portfolio construction, and translating research experience into interviews—without guaranteeing outcomes. The lab tracks emerging ethics guidance from professional societies and updates study protocols when norms evolve.

mentors.name: Diego Allen
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Diego Allen leads the Allen Lab for Grounded NLP, focusing on language technologies where grounding, attribution, and evaluation honesty are first-class requirements—not optional polish. Research mixes transformer-era modeling with careful dataset governance, human protocols, and targeted stress tests for brittleness. Applications include decision-support assistants, scientific literature workflows, and accessibility tools with explicit uncertainty handling. Open challenges include contamination-aware benchmarking, multilingual fairness, and scaling supervision without amplifying silent biases. Students typically begin with reproduction and error analysis, then move to benchmark proposals; Dr. Dr. Diego Allen emphasizes writing-as-thinking. Courses emphasize NLP evaluation literacy and the sociology of dataset creation. Roadmap themes include retrieval-first architectures, structured reasoning interfaces, and lightweight auditing tools for classroom deployments. The lab maintains explicit policies on authorship credit, noting that meaningful intellectual contributions—not  merely routine engineering—guide ordering conversations. When hardware dependencies arise, students learn budgeting basics, cluster etiquette, and carbon-aware experiment planning as part of responsible systems citizenship. Collaborations extend to neighboring engineering departments, clinics, libraries, and civic partners when projects require domain expertise; students are expected to document assumptions gathered from stakeholders.

mentors.name: Matthew Bailey
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Matthew Bailey directs the Bailey Research Group on Decision Systems, focusing on representation learning pipelines where scale, compute budgets, and evaluation integrity must be co-designed rather than treated as afterthoughts. The research program mixes large-scale training insights with smaller diagnostic studies that isolate failure modes in optimization, generalization, or calibration. Application targets span scientific computing assistants, decision-support tools, and safety-critical settings where uncertainty communication matters as much as raw accuracy. A recurring challenge is separating incidental correlations from stable mechanisms—students are encouraged to treat surprising gains as hypotheses that demand mechanism-level scrutiny. Mentorship emphasizes readable code, experiment manifests, and crisp writeups—skills that transfer regardless of whether a project ends in a publication or a strong technical report. Teaching interfaces include graduate seminars on probabilistic ML and undergraduate courses that foreground debugging culture and scientific communication. Students interested in interdisciplinary projects should expect to spend time understanding domain constraints before proposing model changes. Where feasible, projects publish negative results, ablation catalogs, and limitation sections that later cohorts can build upon without repeating dead ends. Outreach includes mentoring novice programmers in adjacent majors when projects demand interdisciplinary teams. Funding mixes federal grants, industry partnerships, and internal fellowships; undergraduates may contribute to grant reporting artifacts, milestones, and reproducibility supplements when appropriate.

mentors.name: Aaron Gibson
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Aaron Gibson directs the Gibson High-Performance Computing Group, building systems where latency tails, resource fairness, and operational observability are engineered—not bolted on after deployment. The group combines kernel/user-space prototyping with rigorous benchmarking and failure injection; students learn to distrust cached conclusions without flamegraphs. Targets include ML training clusters, edge inference stacks, and campus-scale research platforms requiring reliability. Core challenges include tail latency under contention, safe upgrades in distributed settings, and reproducible performance evaluation. Undergraduates often start with instrumented micro-benchmarks and bug fixes; Dr. Dr. Aaron Gibson emphasizes incremental commits and design docs. Teaching emphasizes OS internals, concurrency reasoning, and performance culture grounded in measurement. Roadmap themes include systems support for large-model training, energy-aware scheduling, and verified rollback tooling. Outreach includes mentoring novice programmers in adjacent majors when projects demand interdisciplinary teams. Where feasible, projects publish negative results, ablation catalogs, and limitation sections that later cohorts can build upon without repeating dead ends.
mentors.preferred_background: Strong systems programming maturity and patience for long debugging cycles; coursework in OS + networking helps substantially.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Aaron Gibson publishes in OSDI/SOSP/NSDI-class venues; Gibson High-Performance Computing Group ships artifacts used by partner research groups.
mentors.advising_style: Issue-tracker driven development; expects design notes before large refactors.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~12–18 hours/week during kernel-heavy phases; schedules adjust around compile/debug cycles.
mentors.lab_focus: Gibson High-Performance Computing Group treats systems research as principled engineering with scientific measurement obligations.

mentors.name: Angela Cole
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Angela Cole directs the Cole Vision & Perception Lab with a focus on 3D scene understanding, integrating learning-based inference with geometric priors when they are trustworthy. Method work spans efficient architectures, training curricula, and post-training calibration for deployed stacks. Applications include assistive technologies, autonomous systems research platforms, and scientific imaging where interpretability and failure visibility matter. Key challenges include domain shift, label noise, and defining realistic evaluation scenarios beyond curated sets. Undergraduates often begin by reproducing baselines on internal datasets, then implement diagnostics (slice analysis, failure clustering); Dr. Dr. Angela Cole emphasizes milestone demos. Teaching bridges classical vision with contemporary deep learning labs emphasizing debugging and visualization. Near-term directions include robust multimodal perception, efficient training for edge devices, and evaluation tooling for long-tail behaviors. International readers are considered default: documentation strives for precise terminology, neutral examples, and careful handling of culturally sensitive applications. Collaborations extend to neighboring engineering departments, clinics, libraries, and civic partners when projects require domain expertise; students are expected to document assumptions gathered from stakeholders. Alumni stay loosely connected through shared Slack archives and annual reunions; returning visitors often co-advise capstone threads tied to earlier codebases.

mentors.name: Rachel Campbell
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Rachel Campbell leads the Laboratory for Reliable Learning and studies learning algorithms that remain reliable when data are biased, partially labeled, or collected under shifting conditions. Recent technical themes include structured latent variables, representation alignment under distribution shift, and practical tooling for reproducible experimentation. Impact is pursued through open benchmarks, reference implementations, and documentation aimed at lowering the barrier for responsible adoption. A recurring challenge is separating incidental correlations from stable mechanisms—students are encouraged to treat surprising gains as hypotheses that demand mechanism-level scrutiny. Undergraduate researchers typically begin with reproduction and auditing tasks, then graduate to proposing extensions; Dr. Dr. Rachel Campbell prioritizes weekly technical checkpoints and written milestone notes. Course connections emphasize bridging mathematical intuition with hands-on labs that mirror research workflows. Near-term directions include richer evaluation for generative systems, improved protocols for human-in-the-loop supervision, and tooling that makes robust experimentation teachable—not only feasible for experts. Community norms emphasize kindness rigor: critiques target claims and methods, not individuals; disagreement is modeled as a productive part of research meetings. Students are encouraged to present at internal seminars before external deadlines, practicing clarity under questions from faculty outside their immediate specialty. The group actively participates in department reading groups, artifact reviews, and undergraduate research symposia so students experience the social norms of scientific criticism—not only isolated coding tasks.
mentors.preferred_background: Students who can translate vague ideas into measurable hypotheses, document failures, and iterate with statistical discipline.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Rachel Campbell advises thesis work spanning theory-informed empirical ML and applied collaborations; alumni have joined both academic labs and industry research teams.
mentors.advising_style: Hands-on debugging sessions early in a project, transitioning to student-led ownership after Milestone 1.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~10–14 focused hours/week during steady semesters; heavier spikes near deadlines are avoided via earlier checkpoints.
mentors.lab_focus: Laboratory for Reliable Learning emphasizes reproducible ML research with measurable claims and collaborative tooling.

mentors.name: Mei Martinez
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Mei Martinez leads the Martinez Algorithms & Theory Group, investigating fundamental computation limits and designing algorithms with provable guarantees under realistic constraints. Research blends classical discrete optimization with modern probabilistic tools and structured instance analyses. Motivating domains include routing, scheduling, learning-adjacent subroutines, and resource allocation where constants and logarithmic factors genuinely matter. Students confront tension between worst-case guarantees and empirical structure—projects clarify assumptions explicitly. Mentorship emphasizes lemma notebooks, proof sketches before details, and seminar-style presentations; Dr. Dr. Mei Martinez coaches undergraduates toward incremental publishable lemmas when appropriate. Teaching stresses mathematical communication and proof hygiene alongside algorithm design patterns. Directions include dynamic graph algorithms under partial observability, approximation schemes for structured clustering, and bridges to ML theory. Funding mixes federal grants, industry partnerships, and internal fellowships; undergraduates may contribute to grant reporting artifacts, milestones, and reproducibility supplements when appropriate. When hardware dependencies arise, students learn budgeting basics, cluster etiquette, and carbon-aware experiment planning as part of responsible systems citizenship.
mentors.preferred_background: Comfortable with rigorous proofs and iterative refinement of definitions; expects clarity over speed.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Mei Martinez publishes in STOC/FOCS/SODA-class venues; Martinez Algorithms & Theory Group welcomes careful undergraduate collaborators on theory-meets-systems bridges.
mentors.advising_style: Weekly proof meetings with collaborative whiteboarding and explicit dependency graphs for lemmas.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~8–12 hours/week for theory-heavy reading and problem sessions.
mentors.lab_focus: Martinez Algorithms & Theory Group foregrounds crisp problem formulation and honest theorem statements.

Applications include assistive technologies, autonomous systems research platforms, and scientific imaging where interpretability and failure visibility matter. Key challenges include domain shift, label noise, and defining realistic evaluation scenarios beyond curated sets. Undergraduates often begin by reproducing baselines on internal datasets, then implement diagnostics (slice analysis, failure clustering); Dr. Dr. Hannah Hall emphasizes milestone demos. Teaching bridges classical vision with contemporary deep learning labs emphasizing debugging and visualization. Near-term directions include robust multimodal perception, efficient training for edge devices, and evaluation tooling for long-tail behaviors. Where feasible, projects publish negative results, ablation catalogs, and limitation sections that later cohorts can build upon without repeating dead ends. Funding mixes federal grants, industry partnerships, and internal fellowships; undergraduates may contribute to grant reporting artifacts, milestones, and reproducibility supplements when appropriate.

mentors.name: Gregory Hayes
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Gregory Hayes leads the Hayes Algorithms & Theory Group, investigating fundamental computation limits and designing algorithms with provable guarantees under realistic constraints. Research blends classical discrete optimization with modern probabilistic tools and structured instance analyses. Motivating domains include routing, scheduling, learning-adjacent subroutines, and resource allocation where constants and logarithmic factors genuinely matter. Students confront tension between worst-case guarantees and empirical structure—projects clarify assumptions explicitly. Mentorship emphasizes lemma notebooks, proof sketches before details, and seminar-style presentations; Dr. Dr. Gregory Hayes coaches undergraduates toward incremental publishable lemmas when appropriate. Teaching stresses mathematical communication and proof hygiene alongside algorithm design patterns. Directions include dynamic graph algorithms under partial observability, approximation schemes for structured clustering, and bridges to ML theory. Funding mixes federal grants, industry partnerships, and internal fellowships; undergraduates may contribute to grant reporting artifacts, milestones, and reproducibility supplements when appropriate. Community norms emphasize kindness rigor: critiques target claims and methods, not individuals; disagreement is modeled as a productive part of research meetings.
mentors.preferred_background: Comfortable with rigorous proofs and iterative refinement of definitions; expects clarity over speed.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Gregory Hayes publishes in STOC/FOCS/SODA-class venues; Hayes Algorithms & Theory Group welcomes careful undergraduate collaborators on theory-meets-systems bridges.
mentors.advising_style: Weekly proof meetings with collaborative whiteboarding and explicit dependency graphs for lemmas.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~8–12 hours/week for theory-heavy reading and problem sessions.
mentors.lab_focus: Hayes Algorithms & Theory Group foregrounds crisp problem formulation and honest theorem statements.

mentors.name: Rachel Ward
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Rachel Ward directs the Ward Systems Research Lab, building systems where latency tails, resource fairness, and operational observability are engineered—not bolted on after deployment. The group combines kernel/user-space prototyping with rigorous benchmarking and failure injection; students learn to distrust cached conclusions without flamegraphs. Targets include ML training clusters, edge inference stacks, and campus-scale research platforms requiring reliability. Core challenges include tail latency under contention, safe upgrades in distributed settings, and reproducible performance evaluation. Undergraduates often start with instrumented micro-benchmarks and bug fixes; Dr. Dr. Rachel Ward emphasizes incremental commits and design docs. Teaching emphasizes OS internals, concurrency reasoning, and performance culture grounded in measurement. Roadmap themes include systems support for large-model training, energy-aware scheduling, and verified rollback tooling. Funding mixes federal grants, industry partnerships, and internal fellowships; undergraduates may contribute to grant reporting artifacts, milestones, and reproducibility supplements when appropriate. The group actively participates in department reading groups, artifact reviews, and undergraduate research symposia so students experience the social norms of scientific criticism—not only isolated coding tasks. The lab tracks emerging ethics guidance from professional societies and updates study protocols when norms evolve.
mentors.preferred_background: Strong systems programming maturity and patience for long debugging cycles; coursework in OS + networking helps substantially.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Rachel Ward publishes in OSDI/SOSP/NSDI-class venues; Ward Systems Research Lab ships artifacts used by partner research groups.
mentors.advising_style: Issue-tracker driven development; expects design notes before large refactors.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~12–18 hours/week during kernel-heavy phases; schedules adjust around compile/debug cycles.
mentors.lab_focus: Ward Systems Research Lab treats systems research as principled engineering with scientific measurement obligations.

mentors.name: Justin Gupta
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Justin Gupta leads the Gupta Language Technologies Group, focusing on language technologies where grounding, attribution, and evaluation honesty are first-class requirements—not optional polish. Research mixes transformer-era modeling with careful dataset governance, human protocols, and targeted stress tests for brittleness. Applications include decision-support assistants, scientific literature workflows, and accessibility tools with explicit uncertainty handling. Open challenges include contamination-aware benchmarking, multilingual fairness, and scaling supervision without amplifying silent biases. Students typically begin with reproduction and error analysis, then move to benchmark proposals; Dr. Dr. Justin Gupta emphasizes writing-as-thinking. Courses emphasize NLP evaluation literacy and the sociology of dataset creation. Roadmap themes include retrieval-first architectures, structured reasoning interfaces, and lightweight auditing tools for classroom deployments. The group actively participates in department reading groups, artifact reviews, and undergraduate research symposia so students experience the social norms of scientific criticism—not only isolated coding tasks. Students are encouraged to present at internal seminars before external deadlines, practicing clarity under questions from faculty outside their immediate specialty.
mentors.preferred_background: Interest in either modeling or tooling for reproducible NLP experiments.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Justin Gupta publishes across ACL/EMNLP/NAACL venues; Gupta Language Technologies Group collaborates with HCI and policy scholars on deployment studies.
mentors.advising_style: Paper-style milestone memos; prefers annotated bibliographies early in a project.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~9–13 hours/week with heavier reading loads early on.
mentors.lab_focus: Gupta Language Technologies Group treats language models as systems requiring measurement programs, not single-score comparisons.

mentors.name: Justin Lewis
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Justin Lewis leads the Lewis Human-Centered Computing Lab, designing interactive systems where human workflows—not model scores—define success. Research mixes iterative prototyping with controlled studies and field deployments when feasible. Domains include education technologies, developer tools, healthcare workflows, and civic participation interfaces. Challenges include generalizing findings across populations and avoiding 'demo bait' without measurable outcomes. Students often begin with literature syntheses and pilot studies; Dr. Dr. Justin Lewis coaches storytelling for mixed-methods results. Teaching emphasizes design rationales, ethics, and statistical humility. Directions include human-AI teaming interfaces, accessibility-first AI assistants, and governance tooling for classroom deployments. Outreach includes mentoring novice programmers in adjacent majors when projects demand interdisciplinary teams. The lab maintains explicit policies on authorship credit, noting that meaningful intellectual contributions—not  merely routine engineering—guide ordering conversations. Accessibility is treated as an engineering constraint: interfaces, tutorial videos, and written instructions aim for inclusive defaults rather than retrofits.
mentors.preferred_background: Interest in combining lightweight engineering with rigorous study protocols.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Justin Lewis publishes in CHI/CSCW/UIST venues; Lewis Human-Centered Computing Lab collaborates with education and clinical partners under IRB oversight.
mentors.advising_style: Studio-style critiques plus structured study milestones; encourages reflective design notebooks.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~8–12 hours/week; study seasons may require coordinated scheduling with participants.
mentors.lab_focus: Lewis Human-Centered Computing Lab emphasizes accountable UX research grounded in participant-centered evidence.

mentors.name: Sara Wilson
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Sara Wilson directs the Wilson Lab for 3D Understanding with a focus on 3D scene understanding, integrating learning-based inference with geometric priors when they are trustworthy. Method work spans efficient architectures, training curricula, and post-training calibration for deployed stacks. Applications include assistive technologies, autonomous systems research platforms, and scientific imaging where interpretability and failure visibility matter. Key challenges include domain shift, label noise, and defining realistic evaluation scenarios beyond curated sets. Undergraduates often begin by reproducing baselines on internal datasets, then implement diagnostics (slice analysis, failure clustering); Dr. Dr. Sara Wilson emphasizes milestone demos. Teaching bridges classical vision with contemporary deep learning labs emphasizing debugging and visualization. Near-term directions include robust multimodal perception, efficient training for edge devices, and evaluation tooling for long-tail behaviors. Career mentoring includes guidance on graduate school timing, portfolio construction, and translating research experience into interviews—without guaranteeing outcomes. Accessibility is treated as an engineering constraint: interfaces, tutorial videos, and written instructions aim for inclusive defaults rather than retrofits. Community norms emphasize kindness rigor: critiques target claims and methods, not individuals; disagreement is modeled as a productive part of research meetings.

mentors.name: Peter Li
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Peter Li directs the Laboratory for Reliable Learning, focusing on representation learning pipelines where scale, compute budgets, and evaluation integrity must be co-designed rather than treated as afterthoughts. The research program mixes large-scale training insights with smaller diagnostic studies that isolate failure modes in optimization, generalization, or calibration. Application targets span scientific computing assistants, decision-support tools, and safety-critical settings where uncertainty communication matters as much as raw accuracy. The group invests heavily in measurement: defining tasks beyond single-number scores, auditing slices, and studying intervention sensitivity. Mentorship emphasizes readable code, experiment manifests, and crisp writeups—skills that transfer regardless of whether a project ends in a publication or a strong technical report. Teaching interfaces include graduate seminars on probabilistic ML and undergraduate courses that foreground debugging culture and scientific communication. Near-term directions include richer evaluation for generative systems, improved protocols for human-in-the-loop supervision, and tooling that makes robust experimentation teachable—not only feasible for experts. Career mentoring includes guidance on graduate school timing, portfolio construction, and translating research experience into interviews—without guaranteeing outcomes. Community norms emphasize kindness rigor: critiques target claims and methods, not individuals; disagreement is modeled as a productive part of research meetings.
mentors.preferred_background: Students who can translate vague ideas into measurable hypotheses, document failures, and iterate with statistical discipline.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Peter Li advises thesis work spanning theory-informed empirical ML and applied collaborations; alumni have joined both academic labs and industry research teams.
mentors.advising_style: Explicit expectations around timelines, risk disclosure, and proactive communication when experiments stall.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~12–16 hours/week when approaching a submission-quality milestone (by mutual agreement).
mentors.lab_focus: Laboratory for Reliable Learning emphasizes reproducible ML research with measurable claims and collaborative tooling.

mentors.name: Brooke Peterson
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Brooke Peterson leads the Peterson Lab for Discrete Structures & Optimization, investigating fundamental computation limits and designing algorithms with provable guarantees under realistic constraints. Research blends classical discrete optimization with modern probabilistic tools and structured instance analyses. Motivating domains include routing, scheduling, learning-adjacent subroutines, and resource allocation where constants and logarithmic factors genuinely matter. Students confront tension between worst-case guarantees and empirical structure—projects clarify assumptions explicitly. Mentorship emphasizes lemma notebooks, proof sketches before details, and seminar-style presentations; Dr. Dr. Brooke Peterson coaches undergraduates toward incremental publishable lemmas when appropriate. Teaching stresses mathematical communication and proof hygiene alongside algorithm design patterns. Directions include dynamic graph algorithms under partial observability, approximation schemes for structured clustering, and bridges to ML theory. Collaborations extend to neighboring engineering departments, clinics, libraries, and civic partners when projects require domain expertise; students are expected to document assumptions gathered from stakeholders. Outreach includes mentoring novice programmers in adjacent majors when projects demand interdisciplinary teams. When hardware dependencies arise, students learn budgeting basics, cluster etiquette, and carbon-aware experiment planning as part of responsible systems citizenship.
mentors.preferred_background: Comfortable with rigorous proofs and iterative refinement of definitions; expects clarity over speed.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Brooke Peterson publishes in STOC/FOCS/SODA-class venues; Peterson Lab for Discrete Structures & Optimization welcomes careful undergraduate collaborators on theory-meets-systems bridges.

Applications span campus infrastructure hardening, privacy-preserving analytics, and secure ML deployment pipelines. Where feasible, projects publish negative results, ablation catalogs, and limitation sections that later cohorts can build upon without repeating dead ends.

mentors.name: Harper Baker
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Harper Baker leads the Baker Lab for Adaptive ML and studies learning algorithms that remain reliable when data are biased, partially labeled, or collected under shifting conditions. The research program mixes large-scale training insights with smaller diagnostic studies that isolate failure modes in optimization, generalization, or calibration. Impact is pursued through open benchmarks, reference implementations, and documentation aimed at lowering the barrier for responsible adoption. Another emphasis is compute-aware research: understanding when scaling helps, when it obscures bugs, and how to prioritize experiments under deadline pressure. Mentorship emphasizes readable code, experiment manifests, and crisp writeups—skills that transfer regardless of whether a project ends in a publication or a strong technical report. Course connections emphasize bridging mathematical intuition with hands-on labs that mirror research workflows. Students interested in interdisciplinary projects should expect to spend time understanding domain constraints before proposing model changes. Funding mixes federal grants, industry partnerships, and internal fellowships; undergraduates may contribute to grant reporting artifacts, milestones, and reproducibility supplements when appropriate. International readers are considered default: documentation strives for precise terminology, neutral examples, and careful handling of culturally sensitive applications. The group actively participates in department reading groups, artifact reviews, and undergraduate research symposia so students experience the social norms of scientific criticism—not only isolated coding tasks.
mentors.preferred_background: Students who can translate vague ideas into measurable hypotheses, document failures, and iterate with statistical discipline.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Harper Baker advises thesis work spanning theory-informed empirical ML and applied collaborations; alumni have joined both academic labs and industry research teams.
mentors.advising_style: Hands-on debugging sessions early in a project, transitioning to student-led ownership after Milestone 1.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~8–12 hours/week for coursework-heavy terms, with flexible scheduling around exams.
mentors.lab_focus: Baker Lab for Adaptive ML balances ambitious modeling ideas with mundane-but-critical validation discipline.

mentors.name: James Griffin
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. James Griffin leads the Center-scale ML Research Studio (Griffin) and studies learning algorithms that remain reliable when data are biased, partially labeled, or collected under shifting conditions. Methodologically, the group combines modern deep architectures with careful probabilistic framing, stress-testing claims through synthetic controls and targeted interventions rather than leaderboard chasing alone. The lab collaborates with campus partners on climate modeling workflows, biomedical signal pipelines, and education technologies where data governance is non-negotiable. Another emphasis is compute-aware research: understanding when scaling helps, when it obscures bugs, and how to prioritize experiments under deadline pressure. Undergraduate researchers typically begin with reproduction and auditing tasks, then graduate to proposing extensions; Dr. Dr. James Griffin prioritizes weekly technical checkpoints and written milestone notes. Course connections emphasize bridging mathematical intuition with hands-on labs that mirror research workflows. Near-term directions include richer evaluation for generative systems, improved protocols for human-in-the-loop supervision, and tooling that makes robust experimentation teachable—not only feasible for experts. Accessibility is treated as an engineering constraint: interfaces, tutorial videos, and written instructions aim for inclusive defaults rather than retrofits. Alumni stay loosely connected through shared Slack archives and annual reunions; returning visitors often co-advise capstone threads tied to earlier codebases. Collaborations extend to neighboring engineering departments, clinics, libraries, and civic partners when projects require domain expertise; students are expected to document assumptions gathered from stakeholders.
mentors.preferred_background: Prior coursework in ML + algorithms; interest in either theory-backed empirical work or careful systems-aware ML research.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. James Griffin has published extensively in top ML venues; the Center-scale ML Research Studio (Griffin) routinely releases artifacts and participates in community benchmarking efforts.
mentors.advising_style: Structured weekly meetings with rolling agendas; prefers shared notebooks and documented experiment plans.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~10–14 focused hours/week during steady semesters; heavier spikes near deadlines are avoided via earlier checkpoints.

mentors.name: Arjun Hayes
mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Arjun Hayes directs the Imaging Intelligence Group (Hayes) with a focus on 3D scene understanding, integrating learning-based inference with geometric priors when they are trustworthy. The group combines modern representation learning with classical geometry and careful dataset audits; students learn to question shortcuts like overly tidy benchmarks. Applications include assistive technologies, autonomous systems research platforms, and scientific imaging where interpretability and failure visibility matter. Key challenges include domain shift, label noise, and defining realistic evaluation scenarios beyond curated sets. Undergraduates often begin by reproducing baselines on internal datasets, then implement diagnostics (slice analysis, failure clustering); Dr. Dr. Arjun Hayes emphasizes milestone demos. Teaching bridges classical vision with contemporary deep learning labs emphasizing debugging and visualization. Near-term directions include robust multimodal perception, efficient training for edge devices, and evaluation tooling for long-tail behaviors. Students are encouraged to present at internal seminars before external deadlines, practicing clarity under questions from faculty outside their immediate specialty. When hardware dependencies arise, students learn budgeting basics, cluster etiquette, and carbon-aware experiment planning as part of responsible systems citizenship.
mentors.preferred_background: Strong linear algebra and comfort with probability; willingness to build data pipelines and sanity-check visual outputs.
mentors.experience_summary: Dr. Arjun Hayes publishes in CVPR/ICCV-style venues; Imaging Intelligence Group (Hayes) regularly releases datasets and reference training scripts.
mentors.advising_style: Biweekly deep dives plus asynchronous code review; prefers demo-driven progress checks.
mentors.weekly_time_expectation: Expect ~10–14 hours/week depending on dataset construction phases.
mentors.lab_focus: Imaging Intelligence Group (Hayes) focuses on perception stacks where visual correctness must be validated, not assumed.

mentors.profile_text: Dr. Dr. Justin Gonzalez leads the Gonzalez Algorithms & Theory Group, investigating fundamental computation limits and designing algorithms with provable guarantees under realistic constraints. Research blends classical discrete optimization with modern probabilistic tools and structured instance analyses. Motivating domains include routing, scheduling, learning-adjacent subroutines, and resource allocation where constants and logarithmic factors genuinely matter. Students confront tension between worst-case guarantees and empirical structure—projects clarify assumptions explicitly. Mentorship emphasizes lemma notebooks, proof sketches before details, and seminar-style presentations; Dr. Dr. Justin Gonzalez coaches undergraduates toward incremental publishable lemmas when appropriate. Teaching stresses mathematical communication and proof hygiene alongside algorithm design patterns. Directions include dynamic graph algorithms under partial observability, approximation schemes for structured clustering, and bridges to ML theory. Community norms emphasize kindness rigor: critiques target claims and methods, not individuals; disagreement is modeled as a productive part of research meetings. mentors.profile_text_for_embedding:
Justin Gonzalez
School of Computer Science
Research areas: approximation algorithms; algorithms; randomized algorithms
Keywords: probabilistic inequalities; spectral methods; lower bounds; amortized analysis; matchings; dynamic graphs; streaming algorithms
Dr. Dr. Justin Gonzalez leads the Gonzalez Algorithms & Theory Group, investigating fundamental computation limits and designing algorithms with provable guarantees under realistic constraints. Research blends classical discrete optimization with modern probabilistic tools and structured instance analyses. Motivating domains include routing, scheduling, learning-adjacent subroutines, and resource allocation where constants and logarithmic factors genuinely matter. Students confront tension between worst-case guarantees and empirical structure—projects clarify assumptions explicitly. Mentorship emphasizes lemma notebooks, proof sketches before details, and seminar-style presentations; Dr. Dr. Justin Gonzalez coaches undergraduates toward incremental publishable lemmas when appropriate. Teaching stresses mathematical communication and proof hygiene alongside algorithm design patterns. Directions include dynamic graph algorithms under partial observability, approximation schemes for structured clustering, and bridges to ML theory. Community norms emphasize kindness rigor: critiques target claims and methods, not individuals; disagreement is modeled as a productive part of research meetings. Students are encouraged to present at internal seminars before external deadlines, practicing clarity under questions from faculty outside their immediate specialty.
